intro: Let's start by understanding what happened in your 2025 tax year. This helps us focus only on the sections that matter for your situation.

screens.title: Which of the following happened in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Don't worry if you are unsure — we'll ask follow-up questions for each. We'll cover standard items like RRSP, TFSA, and common credits regardless of your selections.

options.label: I moved to a new city or province
I changed jobs or started a new job
I got married, separated, or divorced
I had a child or adopted
I started a business or became self-employed
I retired or started receiving pension income
I turned 18, 65, or 71 this year
I or a dependent has a disability
I returned to school or a family member attended post-secondary
I purchased my first home
None of the above — straightforward employment year

life_event_moved.message: Relocation noted — we will check moving expense eligibility under s.62(1).

life_event_changed_job.message: Job change noted — we will review your T4 situation and any transition items.

life_event_marriage_change.message: Marital status change noted — we will cover support payments, spousal credits, and property transfers.

life_event_had_child.message: New child noted — we will cover child care expenses, Canada Child Benefit, and RESP.

life_event_started_business.message: Self-employment noted — we will cover business income, expenses, GST/HST, and CPP obligations.

life_event_retired.message: Retirement noted — we will cover pension splitting, RRIF rules, OAS, and the Age Amount.

life_event_age_milestone.message: Age milestone noted — we will confirm what opens or closes at this age for registered plans and credits.

life_event_disability.message: Disability noted — we will cover the Disability Tax Credit, RDSP, medical expenses, and attendant care.

life_event_education.message: Education noted — we will cover tuition credits, student loan interest, and RESP withdrawals.

life_event_first_home.message: First home purchase noted — we will cover FHSA, Home Buyers Plan, and the First Home Buyers Tax Credit.

life_event_none.message: Straightforward year noted. We will still cover the standard deductions and credits that apply to everyone.

branch_completion_summary.message: Got it. Based on your selections we will cover the sections relevant to your 2025 return.
branch_completion_summary.transition: Let us start with your personal profile.

intro: First, a few basics about your situation. These help us personalize everything that follows.

screens.title: Where did you live on December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: This determines your provincial tax rates and province-specific credits. Ontario residents have access to the Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB), LIFT Credit, and Senior Homeowners Property Tax Grant.

options.label: Ontario
British Columbia
Alberta
Quebec
Other province or territory
I became a Canadian resident in 2025

ON.message: Ontario residents have access to the Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB) under OTA s.103.1, the LIFT Credit under OTA s.103.4, and the Senior Homeowners Property Tax Grant under OTA s.104.1 — we will cover all of these at the end.

BC.message: British Columbia provincial credits will be noted where applicable. This tool is optimized for Ontario — all federal items apply to your province.

AB.message: Alberta provincial credits will be noted where applicable. This tool is optimized for Ontario — all federal items apply to your province.

QC.message: Quebec residents file a separate provincial return with Revenu Quebec. This tool covers federal T1 items. Quebec-specific items should be reviewed with a Quebec-licensed preparer.

other_province.message: Provincial credits for your province will be noted where applicable. This tool is optimized for Ontario — all federal items apply to your province.

newcomer.message: Welcome to Canada. As a new resident, your Canadian tax filing covers only the period from your arrival date onward. We will factor this in throughout the interview.

screens.title: What was your marital status on December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: CRA determines most credits based on your status on December 31. If your status changed during 2025, we will ask about that separately in the Marital Status section.

options.label: Single
Married
Common-law partner
Separated
Divorced
Widowed

married.message: Your spouse's net income affects several credits and deductions. We will ask for their income range when relevant.

common_law.message: Common-law partners are treated the same as married couples under the ITA after 12 months of cohabitation or immediately if you have a child together.

separated.message: If you separated in 2025, support payment rules, the eligible dependant amount, and several credit eligibility rules may have changed mid-year. We will cover this in the Marital Status section.

divorced.message: Divorce finalization affects support payment deductibility, credit eligibility, and property disposition rules. We will cover each area.

widowed.message: We are sorry for your loss. The death of a spouse triggers specific rules around RRSP rollovers under s.70(6), capital property, and survivor benefits. We will flag the key items.

screens.title: What is your age as of December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: Age affects registered plan rules, the Age Amount credit under s.118(2), and pension income splitting eligibility under s.60.03.

options.label: Under 18
72 or older

under_18.message: Under 18 noted. If you have employment or other income you are still required to file a return.

65_to_71.message: At 65 or older you may be eligible for the Age Amount credit under s.118(2) worth up to $1,259 federally, the Pension Income Credit under s.118(3), and pension income splitting under s.60.03. We will cover all of these.

over_71.message: At 72 or older your RRSP must have been converted to a RRIF or annuity by December 31 of the year you turned 71 under s.146(2)(b.4). We will verify this — failure to convert results in the full RRSP balance being included in the following year's income.

screens.title: Do you have any dependents?
screens.instruction: Children, elderly parents, or other family members you financially support. This unlocks the Children and Dependents section even if you did not select it as a life event.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Dependents can unlock several credits and deductions including the Caregiver Amount under s.118(1)(c.1), child care expenses under s.63(1), and the eligible dependant amount under s.118(1)(b). We will ask for details in the relevant sections.

false.message: No dependents noted.

branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we will cover your employment income.

intro: Let's cover your employment income for 2025. This includes any job where you received a T4 slip.

screens.title: How many employers did you work for in 2025?
screens.instruction: Include part-time, casual, and seasonal employment. Each employer issues a separate T4.

options.label: I had no employment income in 2025
One employer
Two employers
Three or more employers

none.message: No T4 income noted. We will cover other income sources in the next section.

one.message: One T4 to report. We will check for any employment expenses you may be entitled to deduct.

two.message: Two T4s to report. If you changed jobs mid-year you may have had CPP or EI over-deducted — we will flag this for your preparer.

three_plus.message: Multiple T4s noted. CPP and EI over-deduction is common with multiple employers — your preparer will reconcile this and you may be entitled to a refund of excess premiums.

checklist_update.note: Multiple T4s — confirm CPP/EI over-deduction with preparer

screens.title: Did you work for the full year in Canada?
screens.instruction: This affects your RRSP contribution room, LIFT Credit eligibility, and several credit calculations.

options.label: Yes, I worked in Canada for the full year
No, I arrived or started working part-way through 2025

true.message: Full year Canadian employment confirmed.

false.message: Partial-year employment noted. Your RRSP contribution room, LIFT eligibility, and several credits are based on your Canadian income only. We will flag this throughout.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm arrival date and Canadian employment start date for proration calculations

screens.title: Approximately what was your total employment income in 2025?
screens.instruction: An estimate is fine — this helps us determine which credits and deductions apply to you. We do not store this information.

options.label: Under $20,000
I'd prefer not to say

under_20k.message: At this income level you may qualify for the LIFT Credit (Ontario) under OTA s.103.4, Canada Workers Benefit under s.122.7, and GST/HST Credit under s.122.5. We will confirm each.

20k_to_30k.message: At this income level the LIFT Credit likely applies and the Canada Workers Benefit may apply depending on family income.

30k_to_50k.message: LIFT Credit phase-out begins around $30,000 individual income under OTA s.103.4. We will flag it as possible for your preparer to confirm.

50k_to_100k.message: Good. This helps us calibrate your RRSP deduction benefit and applicable credits.

over_100k.message: Noted. At this income level several phase-out thresholds apply — OAS clawback under s.180.2 if applicable, Age Amount reduction under s.118(2), and others. We will flag each.

prefer_not_to_say.message: No problem. We will flag all income-sensitive items as possible — your preparer will confirm which apply.

screens.title: Did you receive employment income from a foreign employer in 2025?
screens.instruction: This includes income paid in a foreign currency or from a company based outside Canada.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Foreign employment income is fully taxable in Canada under s.5(1) and must be converted to Canadian dollars using the Bank of Canada exchange rate. A foreign tax credit under s.126 may offset taxes paid abroad.

preparer_flag.flag: Obtain foreign income slips and proof of foreign tax paid

false.message: No foreign employment income noted.

screens.title: Did you work from home in 2025?
screens.instruction: If your employer required you to work from home for any period you may be able to deduct home office expenses under s.8(13). A Form T2200 from your employer is required.

options.label: Yes — my employer required me to work from home
Yes — by my own choice
No

yes_required.message: You may be eligible to deduct home office expenses under s.8(13). Your employer must complete Form T2200 under s.8(10) certifying the requirement. Eligible expenses include a proportionate share of rent, utilities, and internet.

yes_choice.message: Home office expenses are only deductible if your employer required you to work from home and certifies this on Form T2200. Working from home by choice does not qualify under s.8(13).

false.message: No home office claim required.

screens.title: Has your employer provided or agreed to sign Form T2200?
screens.instruction: Form T2200 — Declaration of Conditions of Employment — is required by CRA under s.8(10) to support home office and vehicle expense claims.

options.label: Yes, I have it or will obtain it
No, I have not asked yet
I am not sure my employer will sign it

true.message: Form T2200 confirmed. Home office and vehicle expenses remain on your checklist.

document_checklist_add.document: Form T2200 — Declaration of Conditions of Employment signed by employer

false.message: Request Form T2200 from your employer before filing. Without it CRA will deny the deduction under s.8(10). We have flagged this as a priority action.

preparer_flag.flag: Form T2200 not yet obtained — request from employer before filing. CRA will deny claim without it.

unsure.message: Your employer is legally required to sign T2200 if they required you to work from home. We have flagged this for follow-up.

preparer_flag.flag: T2200 status uncertain — confirm employer obligation and obtain before filing

screens.title: Did you use your personal vehicle for work in 2025?
screens.instruction: This excludes regular commuting to and from work — that is not deductible. Eligible use includes client visits, job sites, or travel between work locations.

options.label: Yes — I drove for work purposes such as client visits, job sites, or between locations
No

true.message: Employment vehicle expenses may be deductible under s.8(1)(h.1). Your employer must certify this on Form T2200 under s.8(10). Keep a mileage log — CRA frequently audits vehicle claims.

document_checklist_add.document: Mileage log for 2025 — date, destination, purpose, km per trip, total annual km and business km

false.message: No vehicle expense claim noted.

screens.title: Did you pay any of these work-related expenses out of pocket in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Your employer must not have reimbursed these costs.

options.label: Union or professional association dues
Tools or equipment required for work
Office supplies purchased for work
Professional licensing or registration fees
None of the above

union_dues.message: Union dues are deductible under s.8(1)(i) — they should already appear on your T4 in Box 44. Confirm with your preparer if the amount looks correct.

tools.message: Tradespersons and apprentices may deduct tool costs under s.8(1)(s) and s.8(1)(r). Limits apply — we will include this on your checklist.

supplies.message: Supplies are deductible under s.8(1)(i)(iii) if your employer required you to pay for them and certifies this on Form T2200.

licensing.message: Professional licensing fees required for your occupation are deductible under s.8(1)(i)(iv).

branch_completion_summary.message: Employment income section complete. Here is what we have identified so far.
highlighted_documents: All T4 slips from each employer
Form T2200 if home office or vehicle expenses claimed
Mileage log if vehicle expenses claimed
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: Other Income Sources — pensions, support payments, RRSP withdrawals, and more.

intro: Beyond your T4 employment income, there are many other amounts that must be reported on your tax return. Some arrive as slips, others you may need to track yourself. Let's make sure nothing is missed.

screens.title: Did you receive CPP or OAS payments in 2025?
screens.instruction: Canada Pension Plan and Old Age Security payments are taxable income under s.56(1)(a). You will receive a T4A(P) for CPP and a T4A(OAS) for OAS.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: CPP and OAS payments are taxable under s.56(1)(a). You should receive a T4A(P) and T4A(OAS) slip by late February. Up to 50% of CPP income may be shareable with a spouse for income splitting under s.60.03.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A(P) — CPP Statement of Benefits and T4A(OAS) — OAS Statement of Benefits

false.message: No CPP or OAS income noted.

screens.title: Did you receive pension payments from a former employer's pension plan in 2025?
screens.instruction: This includes defined benefit or defined contribution pension payments. You will receive a T4A slip.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Employer pension income is taxable under s.56(1)(a) and reported on a T4A slip. Eligible pension income qualifies for the Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) and up to 50% may be split with a spouse under s.60.03.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A slip — Statement of Pension Retirement Annuity and Other Income from former employer

false.message: No employer pension noted.

screens.title: Did you receive pension income from another country in 2025?
screens.instruction: This includes pensions from a former employer abroad, government pensions from another country, or US Social Security payments.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Foreign pension income is taxable in Canada under s.56(1)(a) and must be converted to Canadian dollars. Tax treaties may reduce withholding in the source country and a foreign tax credit under s.126 may apply.

preparer_flag.flag: Obtain foreign pension statement and proof of foreign tax withheld. Confirm applicable tax treaty.

false.message: No foreign pension noted.

screens.title: Did you receive Employment Insurance (EI) benefits in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes regular EI, maternity, parental, sickness, or compassionate care benefits. You will receive a T4E slip.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: EI benefits are taxable under s.56(1)(a)(iv) and reported on a T4E slip. If your net income exceeds approximately $79,000 a portion of EI may be repayable.

document_checklist_add.document: T4E — Statement of Employment Insurance and Other Benefits

false.message: No EI benefits noted.

screens.title: Did you withdraw any funds from an RRSP in 2025?
screens.instruction: Any withdrawal from an RRSP — other than under the Home Buyers Plan or Lifelong Learning Plan — is fully taxable as income in the year withdrawn under s.56(1)(h).

options.label: Yes — a regular withdrawal
Yes — under the Home Buyers Plan (HBP)
Yes — under the Lifelong Learning Plan (LLP)
No withdrawals

yes_regular.message: RRSP withdrawals are fully taxable under s.56(1)(h). You will receive a T4RSP slip. Withholding tax is deducted at source but may not cover your full tax liability.

document_checklist_add.docent: T4RSP — Statement of RRSP Income showing withdrawal amount and withholding tax

yes_hbp.message: HBP withdrawals are not taxable when used to purchase a qualifying home under s.146.01. However annual repayments are required starting the second year after withdrawal. If the repayment is missed that year's required amount is added to your income.

yes_llp.message: LLP withdrawals are not taxable while you are enrolled in qualifying education under s.146.02. Repayments begin after graduation. Missed repayments are added to your income for that year.

false.message: No RRSP withdrawals noted.

screens.title: Did you receive spousal or child support payments in 2025?
screens.instruction: Spousal support received is taxable under s.56(1)(b). Child support received under agreements after May 1, 1997 is generally not taxable.

options.label: Yes — spousal support only
Yes — child support only
Yes — both spousal and child support
No

spousal_only.message: Spousal support received able under s.56(1)(b) if paid periodically under a court order or written agreement. Bring your separation agreement or court order.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order and proof of spousal support payments received

child_only.message: Child support received under agreements made after May 1, 1997 is not taxable. No income to report. Bring your agreement to confirm the date.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm agreement date — pre-1997 child support rules differ

both.message: Spousal support is taxable, child support is not. Your agreement should specify the breakdown. Bring the full agreement.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement showing spousal vs child support breakdown and proof of payments received

false.message: No support payments received noted.

screens.title: Did you receive any investment income in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Exclude amounts earned inside a TFSA or RRSP — those are sheltered.

options.label: Interest from bank accounts, GICs, or bonds
Dividends from Canadian corporations
Dividends from foreign corporations
Soldnvestments, property, or cryptocurrency at a gain or loss
None of the above

interest.message: Interest income is fully taxable under s.12(1)(c). You should receive T5 slips for amounts over $50. Interest under $50 must still be reported even without a slip.

document_checklist_add.document: T5 slips — Statement of Investment Income showing interest earned

canadian_dividends.message: Canadian dividends are taxable but eligible for the Dividend Tax Credit under s.121. Eligible dividends are grossed up 38% on your return. Your T5 slip shows both the actual and grossed-up amounts.

document_checklist_add.document: T5 slips — Statement of Investment Income showing actual and grossed-up dividend amounts

foreign_dividends.message: Foreign dividends are fully taxable with no gross-up or dividend tax credit. Foreign withholding tax may generate a foreign tax credit under s.126.

document_checkli_add.document: T5 or NR4 slips for foreign dividends and proof of foreign withholding tax paid

capital_gains.message: Capital gains and losses must be reported under s.39(1). Bring trade confirmations or statements showing purchase price and sale price for each disposition. Cryptocurrency dispositions are also taxable events.

document_checklist_add.document: Trade confirmations or brokerage statements showing ACB and proceeds for each sale including cryptocurrency

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm ACB for each disposition — especially cryptocurrency where tracking is complex

screens.title: Did you earn rental income in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes renting a room, basement suite, or entire property. Also includes short-term rentals such as Airbnb.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Rental income is taxable as property income under s.9(1). Eligible expenses such as mortgage interest, property tax, insurance, repairs, and utilities may be deductible under s.18(1). If you rented part of your principal residence capital gains implications on future sale should be discussed with your preparer.

document_checklist_add.document: Rental income and expense records for 2025 — all receipts for eligible expenses

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm if rental is part of principal residence — affects future capital gains exemption under s.45(1)

false.message: No rental income noted.

branch_completion_summary.message: Other income section complete. Here is what we have identi so far.
highlighted_documents: All T4A, T4A(P), T4A(OAS), T4E slips
T5 and investment income slips
Trade confirmations for capital gains
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we will cover your deductions and any life event sections that apply to you.

intro: You indicated you moved in 2025. Depending on the reason and distance, your moving costs may be deductible under s.62(1). Let's confirm the details.

screens.title: Why did you move?

options.label: I started a new job or transferred with my employer
I relocated to run or start my business
I moved to attend a college or university
Other reason

employment.message: Moving expenses for employment relocation may be deductible under s.62(1). One more question to confirm.

business.message: Moving to start or run a business may also qualify under s.62(1). One more question to confirm.

post_secondary.message: Students who relocate for school may deduct moving expenses against income earned or scholarships received at the new location under s.62(1). One more question to confirm.

other.message: Moving expenses are deductible only for employment, business, or education relocations. Unfortunately this move would not qualify.

screens.title: How far did you move?
screens.instruction: Was your new home at least 40km closer to your new work or school location than your old home? This is measured by the shortest public road route under the eligible relocation definition in s.248(1).

options.label: Yes — definitely more than 40km closer
I am not sure
No — less than 40km closer

true.message: 40km threshold confirmed. Moving expenses added to your checklist under s.62(1).

unsure.message: Your preparer can verify the 40km rule using your old and new addresses. We have flagged moving expenses as possible — gather your receipts in the meantime.

false.message: The 40km threshold is not met. Unfortunately moving expenses would not be deductible in this case.

screens.title: Did you move to a different province or territory?
screens.instruction: Your province of residence on December 31, 2025 determines which provincial tax rates and credits apply for the full year — even if you moved mid-year.

options.label: Yes — I moved to a different province
No — within the same province

true.message: Provincial change noted. Your ce of residence on December 31 determines provincial tax rates and credits for the full year.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm exact date of provincial move — affects provincial credit eligibility and OTB if moved to or from Ontario

false.message: Same-province move noted. Provincial credits are unaffected.

screens.title: Which moving expenses did you incur in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. This builds your document checklist — we are not asking for amounts. All eligible expenses are defined in s.62(2).

options.label: Travel costs to the new location (gas, flights, meals)
Movingompany or storage costs
Temporary accommodation during the move
Lease cancellation costs at old residence
I sold my old home (realtor fees, legal fees, mortgage penalty)
I purchased a new home (legal fees, land transfer taxes)
Costs to maintain old home while vacant after moving

travel.message: Travel costs are eligible under s.62(2). Keep receipts for gas, meals, and accommodation en route.

document_checklist_add.document: Travel receipts — gas, meals, accommodation en route to new location

movers_storage.message: Moving company and storage costs are eligible under s.62(2).

cument_checklist_add.document: Moving company invoice and storage receipts

temp_lodging.message: Temporary lodging is eligible under s.62(2) for up to 15 days.

document_checklist_add.document: Temporary lodging receipts — up to 15 days eligible

lease_cancellation.message: Lease cancellation costs are eligible under s.62(2).

doment_checklist_add.document: Lease cancellation agreement and payment confirmation

sold_old_home.message: Selling costs of your old home including realtor commissions, legal fees, and mortgage penalties are eligible under s.62(2).

document_checklist_add.document: Statement of Adjustments from sale of old home — shows realtor fees, legal fees, and penalties

bout_new_home.message: Legal fees and land transfer taxes on your new home are eligible under s.62(2).

document_checklist_add.document: Statement of Adjustments from purchase of new home

maintain_vacant.message: Costs to maintain a vacant former residence are eligible under s.62(2) within limits — generally while actively trying to sell.

document_checklist_add.document: Vacant he maintenance receipts — utilities, insurance, property tax

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm duration and whether home was actively listed for sale

screens.title: Did you earn income at your new location in 2025?
screens.instruction: Moving expenses aronly deductible against income earned at the new location in the same year under s.62(1). If your income there was low unused expenses carry forward under s.62(1)(c).

options.label: Yes — I worked there for most of the year
Yes — but only for part of the year after moving
Not yet — I moved late in the year or am still looking for work

full_year.message: Good. Your moving expenses are fully deductible against your new location income subject to the total eligible expenses.

partial.message: Your deduction is limited to income earned at the new location in 2025. Any unused moving expenses carry forward to 2026 under s.62(1)(c).

preparer_flag.flag: Calculate income at new location to determine deduction ceiling and carryforward amount

none_yet.message: If you earned no income at the new location in 2025 moving expenses carry forward automatically and may be deducted in 2026 when you begin earning income there.

preparer_flag.flag: No income at new location in 2025 — carry forward full moving expense amount to 2026

screens.title: Did you have unclaimed moving expenses carried forward from 2024?
screens.instruction: If you moved in late 2024 and had insufficient income at the new location unused expenses carry forward to 2025 under s.62(1)(c).

options.label: Yes
No
I am not sure

true.message: Prior year carryforward moving expenses are deductible in 2025 against your new location income. Bring your 2024 Notice of Assessment which shows the carryforward amount.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms moving expense carryforward amount

unsure.message: Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment — CRA lists carryforward amounts there.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — check for moving expense carryforward balance

false.message: No prior year moving expense carryforward noted.

branch_completion_summary.message: Relocation section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: All moving reipts — travel, movers, storage, lodging
Statement of Adjustments if home was sold or purchased
2024 Notice of Assessment if carryforward suspected
Proof of new work location — offer letter or T4

intro: A ce in marital status during 2025 affects several areas of your tax return — credits, deductions, and how income is reported. Let's work through the details.

screens.title: What changed in your marital status in 2025?

options.label: I got married or entered a common-law relationship
I separated from my spouse or partner
My divorce was finalized
My spouse or partner passed away

married_or_commonlaw.message: Congratulations. From the date of marriage or common-law status your spouse's income affects several credits and deductions including the spousal amount under s.118(1)(a). We will cover the key items.

separated.message: We are sorry to hear that. Separation has several tax implications — support payment deductibility under s.60(b), credit adjustments, and potential property transfers under s.73(1). We will work through each.

divorced.message: Divorce finalization affects support payment deductibility under 60(b), credit eligibility, and property disposition. We will cover each area.

widowed.message: We are sorry for your loss. The death of a spouse triggers specific tax rules around the deceased's assets, RRSP rollovers under s.70(6), and your own credit eligibility. We will flag the key items.

screens.title: Does your new spouse or common-law partner have income of their own?
screens.instruction: Your spouse's net income determines your eligibility for the Spousal Amount credit under s.118(1)(a) and affects several other credits. An estimate is fine.

options.label: No income or very little income
Some income — under $15,705
Income above $15,705
I'd prefer not to say

none.message: You may be eligible for the Spousal Amount credit under s.118(1)(a) — worth up to $2,421 federally in 2025. This reduces as your spouse's income increases and phases oompletely above the basic personal amount.

low.message: The Spousal Amount credit is reduced dollar-for-dollar by your spouse's net income. A partial credit may still apply — your preparer will calculate the exact amount.

moderate_or_above.message: At this income level the Spousal Amount credit is fully phased out. However your spouse's income is still relevant for GST/HST Credit and Canada Child Benefit calculations.

prefer_not_to_say.message: No problem. We will flag the Spousal Amount credit as possible — your preparer will calculate eligibility from your spouse's return.

screens.title: When did your separation or divorce occur in 2025?
screens.instruction: The timing affects which credits you can claim and how support payment deductibility is calculated.

options.label: Early 2025 — before July
Late 2025 — July or after

early_2025.message: For most of 2025 you were separated. Credits such as the Spousal Amount are calculated based on your status on December 31 — so if separated by year-end the spousal credit does not apply for 2025.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm exact separation date — affects credit eligibility and support payment deductibility

late_2025.message: You were coupled for part of 2025. Your preparer will determine which credits apply for the full year versus the period you were together. Bring documentation of the separation date.

preparer_flag.flag: Partial year matal status — confirm exact date for credit proration

screens.title: Did you make spousal or child support payments in 2025?
screens.instruction: Spousal support paid is deductible under s.60(b). Child support paid under agreements after May 1, 1997 is generally not deductible.

options.label: Yes — spousal support only
Yes — child support only
Yes — both spousal and child support
No

spousal_only.message: Spousal support paid is deductible under s.60(b) if paid periodically pu to a court order or written agreement. Lump sum payments are generally not deductible. Bring your agreement and proof of payments made.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order specifying support terms and proof of payments made in 2025

child_only.message: Child support paid under agreements made after May 1, 1997 is not deductible. No deduction to claim. Bring your agreement to confirm the date.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm agreement date — pre-1997 child support rules differ

both.message: Spousal support is deductible, child support is not. Your agreement must clearly separate the two amounts. Bring the full agreement — your preparer will identify the deductible portion.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order showing spousal vs child support breakdown and proof of all payments made

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm breakdown of spousal vs child support in agreement

false.message: No support payments paid noted.

screens.title: Did you incur legal fees related to your separation or divorce in 2025?
screens.instruction: Legal fees paid to establish or enforce a right to support payments are deductible under s.60(o.1). Legal fees for property settlement or divorce itself are generally not deductible.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Legal fees paid to establish or enforce a right to support payments are deductible under s.60(o.1). Legal fees for property settlement or divorce itself are not. Bring your legal invoices — your preparer will identify the deductible portion.

document_checklist_add.document: Legal invoices — itemized breakdown of services related to support vs property matters

false.message: No legal fees noted.

screens.title: Did you transfer property to or receive property from your spouse as part of the separation in 2025?
screens.instruction: Property transfers between spouses on breakdown of marriage have specific tax rules under s.73(1) that differ from normal arm's length transfers.

options.label: I transferred property to my spouse
I received property from my spouse
Both
No property transfers

transferred_to_spouse.message: Property transferred to a spouse on marriage breakdown is deemed disposed at its cost amount under s.73(1) — meaning no immediate capital gain is triggered. The receiving suse inherits your cost base. Your preparer must document the transfer carefully to establish the new ACB.

document_checklist_add.document: Original purchase documents for all transferred property — needed to establish ACB for receiving spouse

preparer_flag.flag: Document ACB of all transferred property — rollover under s.) requires careful record

received_from_spouse.message: Property received from a spouse on marriage breakdown carries over at the transferor's cost base under s.73(1). Your future capital gain will be calculated from their original cost — not the value at transfer. Obtain the original purchase documents from your spouse.

document_checklist_add.document: Original purchase documents from transferring spouse — confirms inherited ACB

preparer_flag.flag: Obtain ACB documentation from transferring spouse — determines future capital gain calculation

both.message: Multiple property transfers noted. Each transfer must be documented individually with original cost base. This is an area where profesal review is strongly recommended.

preparer_flag.flag: Multiple property transfers on marriage breakdown — document each separately with ACB

false.message: No property transfers noted.

screens.title: Did your spouse have an RRSP or RRIF at the time of their passing?

options.label: Yes — an RRSP
Yes — a RRIF
Yes — both
No
I am not sure

rrsp.message: If you are named as beneficiary of your spouse's RRSP you may transfer the proceeds to your own RRSP or RRIF tax-free under s.146(8.1). Without this rollover the full RRSP value is included in your spouse's final return. This is a critical planning item — act before the estate is settled.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP account statement at date of death, proof of beneficiary designation, and death certificate

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: Spousal RRSP rollover election must be made before estate settlement — confirm beneficiary designation immediately

rrif.message: RRIF proceeds paid to a surviving spouse may be transferred to your own RRSP or RRIF tax-free under s.146. Minimum withdrawal rules apply going forward. Bring the RRIF statements and beneficiary documents.

document_checklist_add.document: RRIF account statement at date of death, proof of beneficiary designation, and death certificate

preparer_flag.flag: Spousal RRIF rollover — confirm beneficiary designation and minimum withdrawal obligations

both.message: Both RRSP and RRIF noted. Each requires a separate rollover election. Act before the estate is settled.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP and RRIF statements at date of death, beneficiary designations, and death certificate

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: Both RRSP and RRIF rollover elections required — act before estate settlement

false.message: No RRSP or RRIF noted.

unsure.message: This is worth confirming urgently with the financial institution holding your spouse's plan. The rollover election must be made before the estate is settled.

preparer_flag.flag: URGENT: Confirm whether deceed spouse had RRSP or RRIF — rollover window is time-sensitive

screens.title: Did you inherit capital property from your spouse — such as a home, investments, or rental property?

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Capital property transferred to a surviving spouse on death is deemed disposed at its cost amount under s.70(6) — deferring any capital gain until you eventually sell. You inherit the original cost base. Obtain original purchase documents for all inherited property — you will need them when you sell.

document_checklist_add.document: Original purchase documents for all inherited capital property — establishes your inherited ACB

preparer_flag.flag: Document ACB of all inherited capital property — spousal rollover under s.70(6) defers gain but ACB carries over

false.message: No inherited capital prty noted.

branch_completion_summary.message: Marital status section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: Separation agreement or court order if separated or divorced
Proof of support payments made or received
Legal invoices if deducting legal fees
Original property purchase documents for any transferred or inherited assets
RRSP/RRIF statements and beneficiary documents if spouse passed away

intro: Children and other dependents unlock several deductions and credits. Let's make sure you claim everything you're entitled to. Some of these are commonly missed — especially by newcomers and first-time parents.

screens.title: Which of the following describes your children or dependents in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. This determines which deductions and credits are available to you.

options.label: Child under 7 years old at December 31, 2025
Child aged 7 to 16 at December 31, 2025
Child of any age with a physical or mental disability
Adult dependent — parent, grandparent, or other relative I support financially
No children or dependents in 2025

child_under_7.message: Children under 7 attract the highest child care deduction limit — $8,000 per child under s.63(1). We'll confirm eligibility next.

child_7_to_16.message: Children aged 7 to 16 attract a child care deduction limit of $5,000 per child under s.63(1). We'll confirm eligibility next.

child_with_disability.message: A child with a disability qualifies for an $11,000 child care limit and may also be eligible for the Disability Tax Credit transfer to you as parent. We'll cover both.

checklist_preview.label: Disability Tax Credit Transfer (s.118.3(2)) — confirming details

adult_dependent.message: Supporting an adult dependent may qualify you for the Caregiver Amount or Infirm Dependent credit. We'll confirm the details.

screens.title: Are you a single parent — supporting a child without a cohabiting spouse or common-law partner?
screens.instruction: Single parents may claim the Eligible Dependant Amount for one child — worth up to $2,421 federally in 2025.

options.label: Yes — I am the primary supporting parent
No — the other parent is also in the household

true.message: As a single parent you may claim the Eligible Dependant Amount under s.118(1)(b) for one child — this effectively gives you a credit similar to the spousal amount. Note: if you paid child support for this child in 2025 you cannot claim this credit for the same child.
true.preparer_flag: Confirm no child support was paid for the same child claimed as eligible dependant — cannot claim both

false.message: Eligible Dependant Amount not applicable when a cohabiting partner is present. Your partner's spousal amount situation was covered in Branch 1.

screens.title: Did you pay for child care in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes daycare, babysitting, nanny, boarding school, or overnight camps. The child must be 16 or under, or have a disability.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Child care expenses are deductible under s.63(1). The deduction is claimed by the lower-income spouse. Limits are $8,000 per child under 7, $5,000 per child aged 7 to 16, and $11,000 for a child with a disability — capped at 2/3 of the claimant's earned income.

screens.title: Do you have receipts from your child care provider showing their name, address, and SIN or business number?
screens.instruction: CRA requires this information to support the child care deduction. Without it the claim may be denied.

options.label: Yes — I have proper receipts
Some receipts — missing SIN or business number
No formal receipts

true.message: ✓ Receipts confirmed. Child care deduction is well supported.

document_checklist_add.document: Child care receipts — provider name, address, SIN or business number, amounts paid per child

partial.message: Request the missing information from your provider before filing. CRA frequently requests this on audit. Without the provider's SIN or business number the claim is at risk.

preparer_flag.flag: Child care receipts incomplete — obtain provider SIN or BN before filing

false.message: Without receipts the child care deduction is very difficult to support on audit. Contact your provider immediately to obtain documentation. We've flagged this as a priority action.

preparer_flag.flag: No child care receipts — obtain from provider urgently before filing. Claim is unsupportable without documentation.

screens.title: Between you and your spouse or partner, who had the lower net income in 2025?
screens.instruction: Child care expenses must be claimed by the lower-income spouse under s.63(1). This affects whose return the deduction appears on.

options.label: I did — I am the lower-income spouse
My spouse or partner had lower income
Our incomes were similar

self.message: You claim the child care deduction on your return as the lower-income spouse.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm lower-income spouse for child care deduction — claimant must have lower net income

spouse.message: Your spouse claims the child care deduction on their return. Ensure the receipts are provided to their preparer as well.

preparer_flag.flag: Child care deduction claimed by spouse — confirm lower-income status and share receipts with spouse's preparer

similar.message: When incomes are similar your preparer will determine which spouse benefits more from the deduction based on final net income figures.

preparer_flag.flag: Incomes similar — preparer to confirm optimal allocation of child care deduction

screens.title: Do you have or are you considering a Registered Education Savings Plan (RESP) for your child?
screens.instruction: An RESP grows tax-free and attracts the Canada Education Savings Grant of 20% on contributions up to $2,500 annually — a free $500 per year per child.

options.label: Yes — I contributed to an existing RESP in 2025
I opened a new RESP in 2025
I don't have one yet but I'm interested
No

existing.message: RESP contributions attract the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) of 20% on contributions up to $2,500 per year — a maximum grant of $500 annually and $7,200 lifetime under s.146.1. Contributions are not tax deductible but investment growth is tax-sheltered.

document_checklist_add.document: RESP contribution receipt from financial institution

new.message: New RESP noted. Ensure the CESG application is submitted through your financial institution — it is not automatic. The child must be a Canadian resident under 18 to receive the grant.

preparer_flag.flag: New RESP — confirm CESG application submitted through financial institution

considering.message: An RESP is worth opening even with a small contribution — the $500 annual grant requires only a $2,500 contribution to maximize. Unused grant room accumulates and can be caught up in future years up to $1,000 grant per year. Your preparer can advise on timing.

preparer_flag.flag: Taxpayer considering RESP — advise on CESG catch-up opportunity and optimal contribution timing

considering.newcomer_note: Newcomers: CESG eligibility begins from the date your child becomes a Canadian resident — prior years do not qualify but future years do.

screens.title: Does your child or dependent have a Disability Tax Credit (DTC) certificate on file with CRA?

options.label: Yes — Form T2201 has been approved by CRA
Application is in progress
No — not yet applied

true.message: ✓ DTC certificate confirmed. If your child cannot use the full disability amount themselves, the unused portion may be transferred to you as the supporting parent under s.118.3(2). Your preparer will calculate the transferable amount.

document_checklist_add.document: CRA DTC approval letter for dependent — Form T2201 approval

applied.message: DTC application pending. If approved before you file, the credit may apply retroactively to the certificate start date. File your return on time and amend once approval is received if needed.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC application pending for dependent — monitor approval and amend return if approved after filing

false.message: If your child has a prolonged physical or mental impairment that markedly restricts daily activities, they may qualify for the DTC. A medical practitioner must certify Form T2201. The credit can be claimed retroactively up to 10 years once approved — this is worth pursuing.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC not applied for — assess eligibility with medical practitioner. Retroactive claim possible up to 10 years.

screens.title: Are you currently receiving the Canada Child Benefit (CCB) for your child?
screens.instruction: The CCB is a tax-free monthly payment for families with children under 18. It is not taxable income but must be applied for and is income-tested.

options.label: Yes — I receive CCB
I had a new child or arrived in Canada in 2025 and have not yet applied
No

true.message: CCB is not taxable income. However it is recalculated each July based on your prior year net income. Filing your return on time is essential — late filing can interrupt your CCB payments.

preparer_flag.flag: File return on time — CCB recalculated July based on prior year net income. Late filing interrupts payments.

newly_eligible.message: Apply for CCB immediately through CRA My Account or by filing Form RC66. Newcomers must also file Form RC66SCH confirming residency status. Payments are not retroactive beyond 11 months — apply now to avoid losing benefit entitlement.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB not yet applied for — complete RC66 and RC66SCH immediately. Payments not retroactive beyond 11 months.

newly_eligible.newcomer_note: Newcomers must file RC66SCH in addition to RC66 to establish residency status for CCB eligibility.

false.message: If you have children under 18 and have not applied for CCB, this should be done immediately. There may be retroactive entitlement up to 11 months. Your preparer can assist with the application.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB not being received — confirm eligibility and apply if entitled. Retroactive up to 11 months.

screens.title: Did you provide a home for an elderly parent, grandparent, or other infirm adult relative in 2025?

options.label: Yes — they lived in my home
Yes — I supported them financially but they lived elsewhere
No

lived_with_me.message: You may be eligible for the Canada Caregiver Amount under s.118(1)(c.1). Eligibility depends on the dependent's net income and infirmity status. The maximum federal credit is $7,999 for 2025 for an infirm dependent living with you.

preparer_flag.flag: Caregiver amount — confirm dependent's net income and infirmity status. Medical documentation may be required.

document_checklist_add.document: Dependent's income information — net income affects credit calculation

financial_support.message: Financial support of a dependent living elsewhere may qualify for the Infirm Dependent Amount under s.118(1)(d) if the dependent has a physical or mental infirmity. Your preparer will confirm eligibility.

preparer_flag.flag: Infirm dependent living elsewhere — confirm infirmity status and dependency for s.118(1)(d) credit

screens.title: Did you transfer money or property to a child under 18 to invest in their name in 2025?
screens.instruction: Attribution rules may apply — income earned on funds transferred to a minor child is generally taxed in your hands, not theirs.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Attribution rules under s.74.1(2) attribute income earned on transferred property back to you until the year the child turns 18. Capital gains are not attributed — only income such as interest and dividends. Your preparer needs to know the amount transferred and any income earned.

preparer_flag.flag: Attribution rules apply — report income earned on funds transferred to minor child on your return under s.74.1(2)

branch_completion_summary.message: Children and dependents section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: Child care receipts — provider name, SIN or BN, amounts per child
CRA DTC approval letter if disability credit applies
RESP contribution receipt from financial institution
Dependent's income information if caregiver credit applies
RC66 and RC66SCH if CCB not yet applied for
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: if you indicated you started a business or became self-employed, we'll cover that now.

intro: Self-employment income is reported differently from T4 employment income. You report gross revenue and deduct eligible business expenses — only net income is taxed. Let's make sure all legitimate expenses are captured and nothing is missed.

screens.title: Which of the following describes your self-employment in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Different business structures have different reporting requirements.

options.label: Sole proprietor — I operate under my own name or a business name
Partnership — I share the business with one or more partners
Incorporated — I operate through a corporation
Freelance or gig work — contract, platform-based, or occasional
Professional practice — legal, medical, accounting, consulting

sole_proprietor.message: Sole proprietorship income is reported on Form T2125 — Statement of Business Activities. Revenue minus eligible expenses equals net business income, which flows to your T1 return.

partnership.message: Partnership income is reported on Form T2125 using your share of partnership revenue and expenses. A T5013 slip may be issued by the partnership. Bring both the slip and your partnership agreement.

document_checklist_add.document: T5013 Partnership slip if issued, plus partnership agreement showing your profit share

incorporated.message: Operating through a corporation means the corporation files its own T2 return separately. On your personal T1 you report salary drawn (T4), dividends received (T5), and any shareholder benefits. This tool covers your personal T1 — your corporate T2 requires separate preparation.

preparer_flag.flag: Corporation filing required separately — T2 corporate return not covered by this tool. Confirm T4 and T5 slips from corporation.

freelance_gig.message: Freelance and gig income — including platforms like Uber, Airbnb, Fiverr, or contract work — is fully taxable as business income under s.9(1). Even occasional income must be reported. Eligible expenses directly related to earning that income are deductible.

professional.message: Professional practice income follows the same T2125 reporting as sole proprietorship. Note that work in progress (WIP) rules under s.34 allow professionals in certain fields to exclude unbilled WIP from income — your preparer will advise whether this applies.

preparer_flag.flag: Professional practice — confirm WIP election under s.34 if applicable to this profession

screens.title: Approximately what was your gross business revenue in 2025?
screens.instruction: Gross revenue before any expenses. This determines GST/HST registration requirements and affects several calculations.

options.label: Under $30,000
I'd prefer not to say

under_30k.message: Under $30,000 gross revenue you are a small supplier and GST/HST registration is not mandatory — though voluntary registration allows you to claim input tax credits on business purchases. Your preparer can advise whether voluntary registration benefits you.

preparer_flag.flag: Revenue under $30,000 — advise on voluntary GST/HST registration and input tax credit opportunity

30k_to_100k.message: Above $30,000 gross revenue GST/HST registration is mandatory under the Excise Tax Act. If not already registered this must be done immediately — penalties apply for late registration. The Quick Method of accounting may reduce your compliance burden.

preparer_flag.flag: GST/HST registration mandatory above $30,000 — confirm registration status and filing frequency

100k_to_500k.message: GST/HST registration is mandatory. At this revenue level your filing frequency is likely quarterly. Input tax credits on business expenses are recoverable. Bring your GST/HST returns and remittance records.

document_checklist_add.document: GST/HST returns filed in 2025 and remittance records

over_500k.message: At this revenue level monthly GST/HST filing is likely required. Payroll obligations may also apply if you have employees. Bring all GST/HST returns and payroll records.

prefer_not_to_say.message: No problem. We'll flag GST/HST registration as a priority item for your preparer to confirm.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm gross revenue and GST/HST registration status — mandatory above $30,000

screens.title: How did you track your business income and expenses in 2025?
screens.instruction: CRA requires adequate books and records to support your business income and expense claims.

options.label: Accounting software — QuickBooks, Wave, FreshBooks, or similar
Spreadsheet — Excel or Google Sheets
Manual records — paper receipts and notes
I did not keep formal records

accounting_software.message: Good recordkeeping. Export a profit and loss summary for 2025 and bring it to your preparer along with your bank statements for reconciliation.

document_checklist_add.document: Profit and loss report from accounting software — full year 2025

spreadsheet.message: Spreadsheet records are acceptable. Bring a summary of income and expenses by category along with supporting receipts. Your preparer will map these to the T2125 categories.

document_checklist_add.document: Income and expense summary spreadsheet plus all receipts organized by category

manual.message: Manual records are acceptable but riskier on audit. Organize your receipts by expense category before meeting your preparer. Consider accounting software for 2026 to reduce audit risk.

document_checklist_add.document: All business receipts organized by category — income deposits and expense receipts

preparer_flag.flag: Manual records only — organize receipts by category before appointment. Recommend accounting software for 2026.

none.message: Without records CRA can disallow all expense claims on audit and estimate income using bank deposits. Before your preparer appointment, gather: all bank statements, credit card statements, and any receipts you can locate. Your preparer will reconstruct records from bank data.

preparer_flag.flag: No business records — CRITICAL. Gather all bank and credit card statements. Preparer must reconstruct income and expenses from bank data. High audit risk.

document_checklist_add.document: All 2025 bank statements and credit card statements — complete year, all accounts used for business

screens.title: Which categories of business expenses did you incur in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. We will build your document checklist for each category. Only expenses incurred to earn business income are deductible.

options.label: Home office — I worked from home regularly
Vehicle — used for business travel
Advertising and marketing costs
Professional fees — accountant, lawyer, consultant
Office supplies and materials
Business portion of telephone and internet
Meals and entertainment with clients
Business travel — flights, hotels, ground transport
Subcontractors or contract labour
Business insurance
Equipment, computers, or other capital purchases over $500
Salaries or wages paid to employees
None — I had minimal expenses

home_office.message: Home office expenses for self-employed individuals are deductible under s.18(12) — different from the employment home office rules. No T2200 required. You may deduct a proportionate share of rent or mortgage interest, property tax, utilities, and maintenance based on the percentage of your home used exclusively and regularly for business.

document_checklist_add.document: Home office calculation — total square footage of home vs office space, plus rent or mortgage statements, utility bills, property tax bills

vehicle.message: Business vehicle expenses are deductible under s.18(1)(a) based on the proportion of business use. A mileage log is essential — CRA frequently audits vehicle claims. Record date, destination, purpose, and kilometres for every business trip.

document_checklist_add.document: Mileage log for 2025 — date, destination, purpose, km per trip. Total annual km and business km.

meals_entertainment.message: Meals and entertainment expenses are only 50% deductible under s.67.1(1) — even if 100% business-related. Your preparer will apply the 50% limit automatically. Keep all receipts noting the business purpose and attendees.

document_checklist_add.document: Meal and entertainment receipts — note business purpose and attendees on each receipt

capital_purchases.message: Equipment, computers, and other capital assets over $500 are not fully deductible in the year of purchase. They are added to a Capital Cost Allowance (CCA) class and depreciated over time under s.20(1)(a). The Immediate Expensing Incentive may allow full deduction in year of purchase for eligible property — your preparer will confirm.

document_checklist_add.document: Invoices for all capital purchases — date, description, cost, business use percentage

preparer_flag.flag: Capital purchases — confirm CCA class and Immediate Expensing Incentive eligibility for each asset

subcontractors.message: Payments to subcontractors are deductible as contract labour. Keep invoices and payment records. If you paid any individual subcontractor more than $500 in the year, a T4A slip may be required — your preparer will confirm.

document_checklist_add.document: Subcontractor invoices and payment records — name, amount, services provided

preparer_flag.flag: Subcontractor payments — confirm T4A filing obligation for payments over $500 to individuals

salaries_wages.message: If you paid employees in 2025, payroll obligations apply — CPP, EI, income tax withholding, T4 slips, and payroll remittances to CRA. These are separate from your personal T1 return. Bring your payroll records and T4 summary.

preparer_flag.flag: Payroll obligations — confirm CPP, EI, withholding remittances are current. T4 slips due February 28.

screens.title: Is your home office space used exclusively for business?

options.label: Yes — dedicated office space used only for business
No — I use the space for personal purposes as well

true.message: Exclusive business use satisfies the s.18(12) requirement. Calculate the percentage of your home the office represents by square footage — that percentage applies to eligible home expenses.

preparer_flag.flag: Home office — confirm exclusive use and calculate business-use percentage by square footage

false.message: CRA requires the space be used exclusively and regularly for business under s.18(12). Mixed-use spaces are technically ineligible — though in practice CRA accepts spaces primarily used for business on a regular basis. Your preparer will advise on the supportability of this claim.

preparer_flag.flag: Home office — mixed personal and business use. Confirm claim is supportable under s.18(12) exclusive use requirement.

screens.title: Was your net business income before home office expenses a profit or a loss?
screens.instruction: Home office expenses cannot create or increase a business loss under s.18(12). Unused amounts carry forward.

options.label: Profit — revenue exceeded expenses before home office
Approximately break-even
Loss — expenses exceeded revenue

profit.message: Home office expenses are fully deductible against your business profit.

breakeven.message: Home office expenses can reduce income to zero but not below. Any unused home office amount carries forward under s.18(12)(b) to future years when the business is profitable.

loss.message: Home office expenses cannot increase a business loss under s.18(12). The full home office amount carries forward to future years when the business is profitable. Your preparer will calculate the carryforward.

preparer_flag.flag: Business loss — home office expenses carry forward under s.18(12)(b). Calculate carryforward amount for 2026.

screens.title: Did you use your vehicle exclusively for business, or for both business and personal use?

options.label: Exclusively for business — no personal use
Mixed — both business and personal use

exclusive_business.message: 100% business use allows full deduction of operating expenses. Your mileage log should reflect zero personal kilometres. CRA is skeptical of 100% business use claims — ensure your log is thorough and credible.

preparer_flag.flag: 100% business vehicle use claimed — ensure mileage log supports zero personal use. CRA audit risk is elevated for this claim.

mixed_use.message: Only the business-use percentage of operating costs is deductible. Your mileage log determines this percentage — total annual km vs business km. For example, 15,000 business km out of 20,000 total km = 75% business use. Lease and CCA deduction limits also apply to passenger vehicles.

preparer_flag.flag: Mixed-use vehicle — confirm business use percentage from mileage log. Apply passenger vehicle limits under s.13(7)(g) and s.67.2 if applicable.

screens.title: Are you aware that self-employed individuals pay both the employee and employer share of CPP contributions?
screens.instruction: This is one of the most significant tax obligations for self-employed individuals — and one of the most commonly missed when setting aside funds through the year.

options.label: Yes — I am aware and have set aside funds
Yes — I am aware but have not set aside funds
No — I was not aware of this

true.message: Good. The self-employed CPP contribution for 2025 is up to $8,860 — both employee and employer shares. The employee half generates a tax credit. The employer half is fully deductible as an other deduction under s.60(e). The enhanced CPP portion is also deductible under s.60(e.1).

aware_not_saved.message: CPP contributions on self-employment income are due with your tax return on April 30. The full amount for 2025 could be up to $8,860 depending on your net income. Your preparer will calculate the exact amount — ensure funds are available before filing.

preparer_flag.flag: Self-employed CPP — taxpayer has not set aside funds. Calculate liability early and advise on payment planning.

false.message: Important — self-employed individuals pay both the employee share (4.95%) and employer share (4.95%) of CPP on net self-employment income up to $71,300 in 2025. Maximum combined contribution is approximately $8,860. This is due with your April 30 tax return. Plan for this now.

preparer_flag.flag: Taxpayer unaware of self-employed CPP obligation — up to $8,860 due April 30. Advise urgently on payment planning.

screens.title: Did you make quarterly tax installment payments to CRA in 2025?
screens.instruction: Self-employed individuals with net tax owing above $3,000 in 2024 or 2025 must pay quarterly installments. Missed installments attract interest.

options.label: Yes — I made installment payments
No installments made
I'm not sure if I was required to

true.message: Installment payments reduce your balance owing at filing. Bring your installment payment confirmation slips or CRA My Account records showing amounts paid.

document_checklist_add.document: CRA installment payment receipts or My Account payment history — all 2025 payments

false.message: If your net tax owing exceeded $3,000 in 2024, you were likely required to make installments in                      2025. CRA charges installment interest if required payments were not made. Your preparer will calculate any interest owing.

preparer_flag.flag: No installments made — confirm if required. Calculate installment interest if applicable under s.161(2).

unsure.message: Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment — CRA includes an installment reminder if payments were required. We've flagged this for your preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm installnt obligation from 2024 NOA — calculate interest if payments were required but not made.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — check for installment reminder section

screens.title: Did your business operate at a net loss in 2025?
screens.instruction: A business loss can be applied against other income in 2025, carried back three years, or carried forward twenty years.

options.label: Yes — expenses exceeded revenue
No — the business was profitable
Not sure yet

true.message: A non-capital loss from business can be applied against all other income in 2025 under s.3(d). If insufficient other income, it can be carried back 3 years or forward 20 years under s.111(1)(a). Note: CRA may challenge losses from activities that appear to lack a reasonable expectation of profit — especially in early years or for hobby-like activities.

preparer_flag.flag: Business loss — determine optimal application: current year, carryback, or carryforward. Assess reasonable expectation of profit if early-stage or hobby-adjacent.

unsure.message: Your preparer will determine the net income or loss position once all revenue and expenses are compiled. We've flagged the loss carryover provisions as possible.

screens.title: Did you have non-capital losses carried forward from prior years?
screens.instruction: Prior year business losses can be applied against 2025 income if the business is now profitable.

options.label: Yes
No
Not sure — I need to check my prior returns

true.message: Prior year non-capital losses plied against 2025 income reduce your taxable income under s.111(1)(a). Bring your prior year Notices of Assessment showing the carryforward balance.

document_checklist_add.document: Prior year Notices of Assessment showing loss carryforward balance — most recent NOA is sufficient

unsure.message: Check your most recent Notice of Assessment â                    CRA lists all carryforward amounts on the last page. We've flagged this as possible.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — check loss carryforward balance on last page

branch_completion_summary.message: Self-employment section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: Profit and loss summary or orgized expense receipts by category
Mileage log if vehicle expenses claimed
Home office square footage calculation and expense receipts if applicable
GST/HST returns and remittance records
CRA installment payment receipts if applicable
Prior year NOA if loss carryforward suspected
Subcontractor invoices and payment records
Capital asset invoices for CCA calculation
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: if you indicated you retired or started receiving pension income, we'll cover that now.

intro: Retirement income has several planning opportunities that are commonly missed — pension splitting, CPP sharing, and the age amount credit among them. Let's make sure your retirement income is structured as tax-efficiently as possible.

screens.title: Which retirement income sources did you receive in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Each source has different tax treatment and planning options.

options.label: Canada Pension Plan (CPP)
Old Age Security (OAS)
Employer defined benefit or defined contribution pension
Registered Retirement Income Fund (RRIF)
Life annuity or fixed term annuity
RRSP withdrawal (not yet converted to RRIF)
Foreign pension or social security
Not yet receiving retirement income

cpp.message: CPP is taxable under s.56(1)(a). You will receive a T4A(P) slip. Up to 50% of CPP income may be shared with a spouse under s.60.03 — we'll cover this shortly.

oas.message: OAS is taxable under s.56(1)(a). You will receive a T4A(OAS) slip. If your net income exceeds approximately $90,997 in 2025, a portion is clawed back through the OAS Repayment under s.180.2.

employer_pension.message: Employer pension income is taxable under s.56(1)(a) and reported on a T4A slip. It qualifies for the Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) and up to 50% may be split with a spouse under s.60.03.

rrif.message: RRIF income is taxable under s.56(1)(h) and reported on a T4RIF slip. RRIF income qualifies for the Pension Income Credit if you are 65 or older, and up to 50% may be split with a spouse.

annuity.message: Annuity payments are taxable as pension income under s.56(1)(d). A T4A slip is issued. Annuity income qualifies for the Pension Income Credit and pension splitting if you are 65 or older.

rrsp_withdrawal.message: RRSP withdrawals before conversion to a RRIF are fully taxable under s.56(1)(h). Note that RRSP withdrawals do NOT qualify for the Pension Income Credit or pension splitting — only RRIF income qualifies. Converting to a RRIF before withdrawing may be more tax-efficient.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP withdrawal before RRIF conversion — confirm whether converting to RRIF first would qualify income for pension credit and splitting.

foreign_pension.message: Foreign pension and social security income is taxable in Canada under s.56(1)(a). Convert to Canadian dollars using Bank of Canada rates. A foreign tax credit may apply for taxes withheld abroad. Tax treaty provisions may also affect withholding rates in the source country.

preparer_flag.flag: Foreign pension — confirm applicable tax treaty and withholding rate. Obtain proof of foreign tax paid.

screens.title: Do you have a spouse or common-law partner?
screens.instruction: Pension income splitting allows you to allocate up to 50% of eligible pension income to your spouse's return — potentially saving significant tax if your incomes differ.

options.label: Yes — I have a spouse or common-law partner
No

true.message: Pension income splitting under s.60.03 allows you to allocate up to 50% of eligible pension income to your spouse. Both spouses file Form T1032 — Joint Election to Split Pension Income. This must be filed with both returns simultaneously. If you and your spouse use different preparers they must coordinate.

preparer_flag.flag: Pension splitting — Form T1032 required on both returns. If spouses use different preparers, coordinate before filing. Optimal split percentage requires both incomes to calculate.

document_checklist_add.document: Spouse's net income estimate — required to calculate optimal pension split percentage

screens.title: Is your pension income significantly higher than your spouse's total income?
screens.instruction: The greater the income difference, the greater the tax saving from pension splitting. An estimate helps us flag the priority level.

options.label: Yes — my pension income is substantially higher than my spouse's total income
Somewhat — my income is moderately higher
Our incomes are roughly similar

significant_gap.message: Pension splitting is highly valuable in your situation. Moving up to 50% of eligible pension income to a lower-income spouse can save thousands annually — both from lower marginal rates and by potentially qualifying your spouse for the Pension Income Credit. This is a priority item for your preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: Significant income gap — pension splitting likely high value. Calculate optimal split to minimize combined household tax. Consider OAS clawback threshold if applicable.

moderate_gap.message: Pension splitting may still provide meaningful tax savings depending on the exact income difference and applicable tax brackets. Your preparer will model the optimal split amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Moderate income gap — model pension split scenarios to confirm tax saving before filing T1032.

similar.message: When incomes are similar, pension splitting provides limited benefit as both spouses are in similar tax brackets. Your preparer will confirm whether any splitting is worthwhile.

preparer_flag.flag: Similar incomes — confirm whether pension splitting provides any marginal benefit. May not be worthwhile.

screens.title: Have you and your spouse applied to share CPP benefits?
screens.instruction: CPP sharing is different from pension splitting. It is administered by Service Canada — not elected on your tax return — and permanently splits your CPP entitlement with your spouse.

options.label: Yes — we have applied and are sharing CPP
No — we have not applied
I'm not sure

true.message: CPP sharing is already in place. Your T4A(P) slips will reflect the shared amounts. Note that CPP sharing reduces the CPP amount available for pension income splitting on your T1 — your preparer will coordinate both.

preparer_flag.flag: CPP sharing active — coordinate with pension splitting election. Confirm T4A(P) amounts reflect sharing.

false.message: CPP sharing is a permanent income splitting mechanism administered by Service Canada — separate from the annual pension splitting election on your tax return. If one spouse has significantly higher CPP, sharing may reduce combined household tax. Your preparer can advise — applications are made directly to Service Canada.

preparer_flag.flag: CPP sharing not applied — advise whether Application for CPP Sharing (ISP1002) is beneficial given CPP entitlement difference between spouses.

screens.title: Were you 65 or older on December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: Taxpayers aged 65 and over are eligible for the Age Amount credit — worth up to $1,237 federally in 2025, subject to income phase-out.

options.label: Yes — I turned 65 or am older
No — I am under 65

true.message: The Age Amount credit under s.118(2) is available to taxpayers 65 and older. The maximum federal credit is $1,237 for 2025 but phases out above net income of approximately $44,325 and is eliminated above $102,925. If your income is too high to use the full credit, the unused portion may be transferred to a spouse.

screens.title: Is your net income above approximately $44,325?
screens.instruction: Above this threshold the Age Amount begins to phase out. If your income is high enough that you cannot use the full credit, your spouse may be able to claim the unused portion.

options.label: No — under $44,325
Yes — between $44,325 and $102,925
Above $102,925 — credit fully phased out
Not sure

under_threshold.message: Full Age Amount credit applies to your return. No transfer required.

in_phaseout.message: Your Age Amount is partially reduced. The unused portion may be transferable to your spouse under s.118.8. Your preparer will calculate the transferable amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Age Amount in phase-out range — calculate unused portion and transfer to spouse if applicable under s.118.8.

above_phaseout.message: Age Amount fully phased out at your income level. However if your spouse is also 65 or older and has lower income, they may still claim their own Age Amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Age Amount eliminated at this income level — confirm spouse's eligibility independently.

screens.title: Is this your first year receiving eligible pension income?
screens.instruction: The Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) is worth up to $300 federally in 2025 — a 15% credit on up to $2,000 of eligible pension income. It is non-refundable but frequently missed in the first year of retirement.

options.label: Yes — first year receiving pension
No — I have been receiving pension for multiple years

true.message: First year of pension income — the Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) applies automatically from the first dollar of eligible pension income. Ensure your preparer applies it — it is sometimes omitted when pension income is small.

preparer_flag.flag: First year pension — confirm Pension Income Credit applied under s.118(3). Non-refundable, up to $300 federal.

false.message: Pension Income Credit confirmed as an ongoing item. Your preparer will apply it as in prior years.

screens.title: Did you turn 71 in 2025?
screens.instruction: The year you turn 71 is the last year you can hold an RRSP. It must be converted to a RRIF, annuity, or collapsed by December 31 of that year.

options.label: Yes — I turned 71 in 2025
No

true.message: RRSP conversion is mandatory by December 31, 2025 under s.146(2)(b.4). If not converted by year-end, the full RRSP balance is included in your 2026 income — potentially a catastrophic tax event. Confirm with your financial institution immediately that conversion was completed.

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: RRSP must be converted to RRIF or annuity by December 31, 2025. Confirm conversion was completed — failure results in full RRSP balance included in 2026 income.

document_checklist_add.document: RRIF or annuity conversion confirmation from financial institution — confirms conversion completed before December 31, 2025

screens.title: Did you make a final RRSP contribution in the year you turned 71 before converting?
screens.instruction: You may make a final RRSP contribution in the year you turn 71 before converting. This is the last opportunity to use any remaining contribution room.

options.label: Yes — I made a final contribution
No — I did not make a final contribution
I'm not sure of my remaining room

true.message: Final RRSP contribution confirmed. Bring your contribution receipt — it is deductible on your 2025 return against your remaining contribution room.

document_checklist_add.document: Final RRSP contribution receipt from financial institution
