unsure.message: Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment for your remaining RRSP contribution room. If room exists, a final contribution before conversion is the last opportunity to use it. Your preparer can advise.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm remaining RRSP room from 2024 NOA — advise on final contribution before RRIF conversion if room available.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — shows remaining RRSP contribution room

screens.title: Did you withdraw at least the minimum required amount from your RRIF in 2025?
screens.instruction: RRIF holders must withdraw a minimum amount each year calculated as a percentage of the January 1 balance. The percentage increases with age. Insufficient withdrawal results in a 1% per month penalty tax on the shortfall.

options.label: Yes — minimum withdrawal was taken
I'm not certain the minimum was met
This is the first year of my RRIF — no minimum required in first year

true.message: RRIF minimum confirmed. Your T4RIF slip will show the amount withdrawn. Remember that any withdrawal above the minimum also qualifies for the Pension Income Credit and pension splitting.

false.message: If the RRIF minimum was not withdrawn in 2025, a penalty tax of 1% per month applies on the shortfall under s.146.3(5). Your financial institution should have issued the minimum automatically — confirm with them immediately if in doubt. Your preparer will calculate any penalty owing.

preparer_flag.flag: RRIF minimum withdrawal uncertain — confirm with financial institution. Penalty tax of 1% per month applies on shortfall under s.146.3(5).

document_checklist_add.document: RRIF statemen showing January 1 2025 balance and all withdrawals made in 2025 — confirms minimum was met

first_year.message: No minimum withdrawal is required in the first calendar year a RRIF is established. Minimum withdrawals begin in the second year. Your preparer will calculate your 2026 minimum based on your December 31, 2025 balance.

preparer_flag.flag: First year RRIF — calculate 2026 minimum withdrawal from December 31, 2025 balance. Advise on optimal withdrawal strategy.

screens.title: Do you expect your net income in 2025 to be above approximately $90,997?
screens.instruction: OAS is clawed back at 15 cents per dollar of net income above this threshold. It is fully eliminated above approximately $148,179 in 2025.

options.label: Yes — likely above $90,997
No — below $90,997
Not sure

true.message: OAS clawback — formally the OAS Repayment under s.180.2 — applies at 15% of net income above the threshold. Income-reducing strategies such as RRSP contributions, pension splitting, or charitable donations may reduce net income below the threshold and preserve OAS entitlement. Your preparer should model this before filing.

preparer_flag.flag: OAS clawback risk — model income reduction strategies before filing. Pension splitting, RRSP contributions, and charitable donations may reduce net income below clawback threshold of $90,997.

unsure.message: We've flagged OAS clawback as possible. Your preparer will calculate net income and assess clawback exposure.

screens.title: Do you have both registered accounts (RRSP/RRIF) and non-registered investments?
screens.instruction: The order in which you draw down registered and non-registered accounts significantly affects lifetime tax. This is a planning conversation worth having with your preparer.

options.label: Yes — both registered and non-registered
No — primarily registered accounts

true.message: Drawdown sequence planning — deciding which accounts to draw from first — can significantly reduce lifetime tax. Factors include OAS clawback threshold, marginal rate brackets, estate planning goals, and TFSA room. This is a strategic conversation for your preparer beyond this year's T1 return.

preparer_flag.flag: Mixed registered and non-registered accounts — advise on drawdown sequence strategy for lifetime tax minimization. Consider OAS threshold, bracket management, and TFSA optimization.

screens.title: Are you 64 or older and do you own your home in Ontario?
screens.instruction: The Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant provides up to $500 annually to eligible seniors who own their home and have income below certain thresholds.

options.label: Yes — age 64 or older and own my Ontario home
No

true.message: Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant provides up to $500 annually under OTA s.104.1. Maximum grant applies below $35,000 individual income and phases out to $50,000. Claimed on your Ontario tax return — your preparer will apply it automatically.

document_checklist_add.document: Property tax bill for 2025 — confirms home ownership anx paid

branch_completion_summary.message: Retirement section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: All T4A, T4A(P), T4A(OAS), T4RIF slips — request from financial institutions by late February
Spouse net income estimate — required for pension splitting calculation
Form T1032 — must be filed on both returns simultaneously if pension splitting elected
RRIF conversion confirmation if you turned 71 in 2025 — critical document
RRIF January 1 balance statement — confirms minimum withdrawal calculation
Property tax bill if Ontario Senior Grant applies
Prior year NOA — confirms RRSP room for final contribution
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we'll cover education expenses and student credits.

intro: Post-secondary education generates several tax credits and deductions — for the student and potentially for supporting family members. Let's make sure tuition credits, student loan interest, and any RESP withdrawals are properly handled.

screens.title: Who attended post-secondary school in 2025?
screens.instruction: This determines whether education credits belong on your return, a family member's return, or both.

options.label: I attended post-secondary school myself
My child or grandchild attended
Both — I attended and so did my child or grandchild
My spouse or common-law partner attended

self.message: Tuition credits belong on your return first. Unused amounts can be transferred to a supporting parent, grandparent, or spouse — or carried forward indefinitely to your own future returns.

dependent_child.message: Tuition credits belong on your child's return first. If your child has little or no tax owing, up to $5,000 of unused credits may be transferred to you as supporting parent under s.118.9.

both.message: Both scenarios apply — we'll cover your own tuition first, then your child's situation.

spouse.message: Your spouse's tuition credits belong on their return first. Unused amounts may be transferred to your return under s.118.8 — or carried forward on their return.

screens.title: Were you enrolled as a full-time or part-time student in 2025?
screens.instruction: Full-time means enrolled in a qualifying education program for at least three consecutive months. Part-time has different scholarship and RESP withdrawal rules.

options.label: Full-time — at least 3 consecutive months
Part-time
Both — I switched between full-time and part-time during the year

full_time.message: Full-time enrollment confirmed. Your scholarship and bursary income is fully exempt from tax under s.56(3). RESP Educational Assistance Payments up to $5,000 are available in the first 13 weeks.

part_time.message: Part-time enrollment noted. Scholarship income is exempt only to the extent of tuition and program costs under s.56(3). RESP EAP withdrawal limit is $2,500 in the first 13 weeks.

both.message: Mixed enrollment noted. Your preparer will apply the appropriate rules for each period. Bring your enrollment confirmation showing dates.

preparer_flag.flag: Mixed full-time and part-time enrollment — apply scholarship exemption and RESP EAP limits separately for each period.

screens.title: Where did you attend school in 2025?
screens.instruction: Canadian and foreign institutions are both eligible for the tuition credit under certain conditions. The institution must be designated by ESDC.

options.label: Canadian college or university
Foreign university or college
Both — courses at Canadian and foreign institutions

canadian.message: Canadian institution tuition is eligible for the tuition credit under s.118.5(1)(a). Your institution will issue a T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate — by the end of February.

document_checklist_add.document: T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate from each institution attended in 2025

foreign.message: Foreign university tuition qualifies under s.118.5(1)(b) if the institution is a university outside Canada and you were enrolled in a course of not less than 13 consecutive weeks leading to a degree. The institution does not issue a T2202 — bring your official tuition receipt showing the institution name, program, and amount paid.

document_checklist_add.document: Official tuition receipt from foreign university — must show institution name, program name, enrollment period, and tuition amount

preparer_flag.flag: Foreign institution tuition — confirm institution qualifies under s.118.5(1)(b): must be a university and course must be at least 13 consecutive weeks leading to a degree.

both.message: Both Canadian and foreign institution tuition noted. Different rules apply to each — T2202 for Canadian, official receipt for foreign. Bring both.

document_checklist_add.document: T2202 from Canadian institution and official tuition receipt from foreign institution

screens.title: Approximately how much did you pay in tuition in 2025?
screens.instruction: Tuition of at least $100 per institution is required to claim the credit. This helps us assess whether a transfer to a parent is worth considering.

options.label: Under $1,000
I'd prefer not to say

under_1k.message: Tuition credit applies at 15% federal rate. At this level the credit value is modest — under $150 federally. Check your T2202 confirms the amount.

1k_to_5k.message: Tuition credit confirmed. At this level the credit is worth $150 – $750 federally. If you have little income of your own, transferring up to $5,000 to a supporting parent may be more valuable — we'll confirm your income situation shortly.

5k_to_15k.message: Significant tuition credit confirmed. Worth $750 – $2,250 federally. If your income is low, the transfer and carryforward decision is important — we'll cover that next.

over_15k.message: Large tuition credit confirmed — worth over $2,250 federally. The transfer and carryforward decision is significant here. We'll work through the options carefully.

screens.title: Did you have significant income of your own in 2025?
screens.instruction: This determines whether you can use your tuition credit yourself or whether transferring to a parent is worth considering.

options.label: Minimal or no income — I had little tax owing in 2025
Some income — enough to have some tax owing
Significant income — I worked substantially alongside school

minimal.message: With minimal income you likely cannot use all your tuition credits this year. You have two options: transfer up to $5,000 of unused credits to a supporting parent, grandparent, or spouse now under s.118.9 — or carry the full amount forward to your own future returns under s.118.61. The transfer benefits your family immediately but permanently reduces your future carryforward. This is worth discussing with your preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: Student has minimal income — model transfer vs carryforward decision. Transfer to parent immediately useful if parent in high bracket. Carryforward preserves student's own future benefit.

moderate.message: With moderate income you may use some tuition credits yourself and transfer or carry forward the remainder. Your preparer will calculate the optimal split.

preparer_flag.flag: Partial tuition credit usage — calculate amount usable by student, amount transferable to parent (max $5,000), and amount to carry forward.

significant.message: With significant income you likely use most or all of your tuition credits yourself. Any unused portion still carries forward to future years.

screens.title: Is there a parent, grandparent, or spouse who financially supported your education in 2025?
screens.instruction: Up to $5,000 of unused tuition credits may be transferred to a supporting person under s.118.9. The student must designate the transfer — it cannot be claimed without the student's consent.

options.label: Yes — a parent or grandparent supported me
Yes — my spouse or common-law partner supported my education
No supporting person — I was self-funded

parent_grandparent.message: Up to $5,000 of your unused tuition credit may be transferred to a supporting parent or grandparent under s.118.9. The transfer is designated on your T2202 or Schedule 11. Both your return and the recipient's return must reflect the same amount — coordinate with the recipient's preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: Tuition transfer to parent/grandparent — max $5,000 under s.118.9. Designate on Schedule 11. Coordinate transfer amount with recipient's preparer. Both returns must reflect same amount.

document_checklist_add.document: T2202 — must be signed by student designating transfer amount to supporting person

spouse.message: Up to $5,000 of your unused tuition credit may be transferred to your spouse under s.118.8. Coordinate with your spouse's preparer — both returns must reflect the same transfer amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Tuition transfer to spouse — max $5,000 under s.118.8. Coordinate with spouse's preparer.

false.message: Unused tuition credits carry forward indefinitely to your future returns under s.118.61. No transfer to make this year.

screens.title: Do you have unused tuition credits carried forward from prior years?
screens.instruction: Unused tuition credits accumulate indefinitely and can be applied in any future year when you have tax owing.

options.label: Yes
No
Not sure — I need to check my prior returns

true.message: Prior year tuition carryforward can be applied against your 2025 tax owing under s.118.61. Bring your most recent Notice of Assessment which shows the carryforward balance.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — shows tuition credit carryforward balance

unsure.message: Check your most recent Notice of Assessment — CRA lists your tuition carryforward balance on the last page. Even small carryforward amounts add up over years of study. We've flagged this as possible.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — check tuition carryforward balance on last page

screens.title: Did you make interest payments on a Canadian student loan in 2025?
screens.instruction: Interest on government student loans — Canada Student Loans, provincial student loans — qualifies for a federal tax credit under s.118.62. Private bank loans do not qualify.

options.label: Yes — on a government student loan (NSLSC or provincial)
Yes — on a private bank student loan only
Both government and private loans
No student loan interest payments

government_loan.message: Interest on government student loans qualifies for a 15% federal credit under s.118.62. Note that as of April 1, 2023, federal student loans no longer accrue interest — so this credit primarily applies to provincial loans and older federal loan balances. Your lender will provide an annual interest statement.

document_checklist_add.document: Student loan interest statement from NSLSC or provincial lender — shows total interest paid in 2025

private_loan.message: Unfortunately interest on private bank loans does not qualify for the student loan interest credit under s.118.62 — only government-issued student loans qualify.

both_loans.message: Only the government loan interest qualifies for the s.118.62 credit. Private loan interest is not eligible. Your NSLSC or provincial lender statement will show the qualifying amount.

document_checklist_add.document: Government student loan interest statement — NSLSC or provincial lender only. Private loan interest does not qualify.

screens.title: Did you have unused student loan interest credits from prior years?
screens.instruction: Unused student loan interest credits can be carried forward 5 years under s.118.62. Unlike tuition carryforward, this has a 5-year limit — use it or lose it.

options.label: Yes
No
Not sure

true.message: Prior year student loan interest carryforward can be applied in 2025. Bring your prior Notice of Assessment confirming the carryforward balance. Remember this credit expires after 5 years — apply it before it lapses.

preparer_flag.flag: Student loan interest carryforward — 5-year limit applies under s.118.62. Confirm expiry year and apply before it lapses.

unsure.message: Check your most recent Notice of Assessment. Given the 5-year expiry, this is worth verifying urgently.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm student loan interest carryforward from NOA — 5-year expiry means this should not be overlooked.

screens.title: Did you or a dependent receive RESP Educational Assistance Payments (EAPs) in 2025?
screens.instruction: EAPs from an RESP are taxable in the student's hands — not the contritor's. The student receives a T4A slip. Tuition and other credits usually offset the tax.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: RESP Educational Assistance Payments are taxable income in the student's hands under s.56(1)(q) — reported on a T4A slip. The good news is that tuition credits usually offset the tax entirely for most students. The original RESP contributions are retued tax-free — only the grant and investment growth portions are taxable.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A slip from RESP provider — shows taxable EAPunt received by student in 2025

preparer_flag.flag: RESP EAP taxable in student's hands under s.56(1)(q). Confirm tuition credits offset tax. Watch EAP limit: $5,000 in first 13 weeks full-time, $2,500 part-time.

screens.title: Did your child receive a T2202 from their institution for 2025?
screens.instruction: The T2202 is issued by the institution and is the key document for the tuition credit and any transfer to you as parent.

options.label: Yes — T2202 received or available online
Not yet — sued by end of February
I'm not sure if my child has it

true.message: T2202 confirmed. The tuition credit belongs on your child's return first. If your child has little or no tax owing, up to $5,000 may be transferred to you under s.118.9. Your child's preparer must designate the transfer amount — coordinate before filing either return.

document_checklist_add.document: Child's T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate. Student must designate transfer amount on Schedule 11.

preparer_flag.flag: Dependent student uition transfer — coordinate child's Schedule 11 designation with parent's return. Max $5,000 transfer. Both returns must reflect same amount.

false.message: T2202 certificates are issued by institutions by the end of Februa. Your child can access it through their student account online. Wait for it before filing either return.

preparer_flag.flag: T2202 not yet received — wait for certificate before filing. Available through stent's online account by end of February.

screens.title: Did you move to attend school in 2025?
screens.instruction: Students who relocate for post-secondary education may deduct moving expenses against income earned or scholarships received at the new location — if they moved at least 40km closer to the institution.

options.label: Yes — I relocated to attend school
No — I commuted from my existing home

true.message: Moving expenses for students are ductible under s.62(1) if you moved at least 40km closer to your institution. Deductible against employment income, business income, or taxable scholarship income at the new location. Note: if you already covered this in the Relocation section, your preparer will consolidate both moves.

preparer_flag.flag: Student relocation — confirm 40km threshold and income at new location to support moving expense deduction under s.62(1).

screens.title: Do you have an outstanding Lifelong Learning Plan (LLP) balance from a prior RRSP withdrawal?
screens.instruction: LLP withdrawals from an RRSP must be repaid over 10 years. If the required annual repayment is not made, that year's amount is added to your income.

options.label: Yes — I have an LLP balance to repay
No
Not sure — I withdrew RRSP funds for school in a prior year

true.message: LLP repayments aue annually starting the fifth year after your first LLP withdrawal or the year after you leave school — whichever is earlier. The annual repayment is 1/10th of your total LLP balance. If not repaid, that amount is included in your income under s.146.02. Check your Notice of Assessment for your LLP balance.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — shows LLP balance and required annual repayment amount

preparer_flag.flag: LLP repayment due — confirm annual repayment amount from NOA. If not repaid, amount added income under s.146.02.

unsure.message: If you withdrew RRSP funds for education in a prior year under the LLP, check your most recent Notice of Assessment — it will show your LLP balance and required repayment amount. Missing a repayment adds to your income — worth confirming.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of sessment — check LLP balance and repayment schedule

screens.title: Did you receive a scholarship, bursary, or fellowship in 2025?
screens.instruction: Already covered in the Other Income section — we are confirming here to ensure tuition credits are coordinated against any taxable scholarship income.

options.label: Yes
No

true.message: Scholarship income is fully exempt for full-time students in qualifying programs under s.56(3). Your tuition credits can offset any taxable portion. You will receive a T4A slip — bring it regardless as your preparer will confirm the exemption.

document_checklist_add.docent: T4A slip — scholarship, bursary, or fellowship income. Bring even if you believe it is exempt.

branch_completion_summary.message: Education section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate from each institution attended. Available through student account online by end of February.
Most recent Notice of Assessment — shows tuition carryforward balance and LLP balance if applicable.
Student loan interest statement from NSLSC or provincial lender.
T4A slip for RESP EAP withdrawals or scholarship income.
Official tuition rpts if foreign institution attended.
Child's T2202 if transfer to parent is being considered — coordinate before filing.
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: if you purchased your first home in 2025, we'll cover that now.

intro: Purchasing your first home triggers several tax benefits — some applied automatically, others requiring specific elections or applications. Let's make sure you capture all of them. This is particularly important for newcomers purchasing their first Canadian home.

screens.title: What is the status of your first home purchase in 2025?
screens.instruction: This determines which items apply to your 2025 tax return specifically.

options.label: I purchased and took possession in 2025
I purchased before 2025 — this is my first full year of ownership
I have a purchase agreement but have not yet taken possession
I am saving and planning to purchase — not yet bought

purchased_2025.message: Purchase in 2025 confirmed. Several credits and deductions may apply on your 2025 return. Let's work through each.

purchased_pre2025.message: If you purchased before 2025, the First Home Buyers' Tax Credit would have been claimed on your prior year return. However HBP repayments may be due in 2025 and FHSA planning may still be relevant if your account was not yet used. We'll confirm the relevant items.

under_contract.message: Purchase agreement in place but not yet closed. Most tax items apply in the year of closing — so they may fall on your 2026 return rather than 2025. However FHSA contributions made in 2025 are deductible on your 2025 return regardless of when you close.

preparer_flag.flag: Home purchase under contract — confirm closing date. Most credits apply in year of closing. FHSA contributions deductible in 2025 regardless.

saving_planning.message: If you are saving for your first home, the FHSA is the most powerful tool available — contributions are tax deductible AND withdrawals are tax-free for a qualifying purchase. We'll cover FHSA contributions you may have made in 2025.

screens.title: Can you confirm you are a first-time home buyer for tax purposes?
screens.instruction: For tax purposes, you are a first-time buyer if neither you nor your spouse owned a qualifying home that you lived in at any time during the current year or the preceding four calendar years.

options.label: Yes — neither I nor my spouse owned a principal residence in the past 5 years
My spouse owned a home in the past 5 years but I did not
No — I or my spouse owned a home within the past 5 years

true.message: First-time buyer status confirmed. You are eligible for the First Home Buyers' Tax Credit, Home Buyers' Plan, and FHSA — subject to individual conditions for each.

spouse_owned.message: If your spouse owned a home in the past 5 years, neither of you qualifies as a first-time buyer for the Home Buyers' Plan or First Home Buyers' Tax Credit. However FHSA withdrawals may still apply if your account was opened when you were first-time eligible.

preparer_flag.flag: Spouse owned home in prior 5 years — confirm first-time buyer eligibility for HBP and FHSA withdrawal. Tax Credit likely not available.

false.message: If you or your spouse owned a home in the past 5 years, you do not qualify as a first-time buyer for the Home Buyers' Plan or First Home Buyers' Tax Credit. FHSA withdrawal eligibility also depends on when the account was opened. We'll flag this for your preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: Not a first-time buyer — confirm eligibility for each first-home benefit individually. Most require no home owned in prior 5 years.

screens.title: Did you contribute to a First Home Savings Account (FHSA) in 2025?
screens.instruction: FHSA contributions are tax deductible up to $8,000 annually — like an RRSP. Investment growth is tax-free and qualifying withdrawals are also tax-free — unlike an RRSP. This is the most tax-advantaged savings vehicle available for first-time buyers.

options.label: Yes — I contributed to an FHSA in 2025
I opened an FHSA but did not contribute in 2025
No — I do not have an FHSA

true.message: FHSA contribution confirmed. Contributions are deductible under s.146.6 up to $8,000 annually with a $40,000 lifetime limit. Unlike RRSP, unused room carries forward only one year — a maximum of $8,000 additional room can be carried forward, giving a maximum of $16,000 in the catch-up year. Your financial institution will issue an FHSA contribution receipt.

document_checklist_add.document: FHSA contribution receipt from financial institution — confirms 2025 contribution amount

opened_not_contributed.message: Opening an FHSA without contributing still activates your contribution room. Contributions made up to March 1, 2026 can be deducted on your 2025 return — same deadline as RRSP. Consider maximizing your 2025 room before the deadline if you have the funds.

preparer_flag.flag: FHSA opened but no contribution — advise on making 2025 contribution before March 1, 2026 deadline to maximize deduction.

no_fhsa.message: If you have not yet opened an FHSA and are eligible, opening one now activates your contribution room from the date of opening. A 2025 contribution up to $8,000 is deductible on your 2025 return if made before March 1, 2026. Your preparer can advise on eligibility.

preparer_flag.flag: No FHSA opened — advise on eligibility and whether opening and contributing before March 1, 2026 is beneficial for 2025 return.

screens.title: Did you withdraw from your FHSA to purchase your home in 2025?
screens.instruction: FHSA withdrawals for a qualifying first home purchase are completely tax-free. A qualifying withdrawal requires a written agreement to buy or build a qualifying home before October 1 of the following year.

options.label: Yes — I withdrew from my FHSA for the purchase
No — I kept my FHSA funds intact

true.message: FHSA qualifying withdrawal confirmed. The withdrawal is tax-free under s.146.6 and does not need to be repaid — unlike the Home Buyers' Plan. Your financial institution will issue a T4FHSA slip showing the withdrawal. You must file Form RC686 — Designation of a Qualifying Withdrawal from an FHSA — with your return.

document_checklist_add.document: T4FHSA slip — FHSA withdrawal amount
Form RC686 — Designation of Qualifying Withdrawal from FHSA — must be filed with return
Purchase agreement — written agreement to buy qualifying home required for FHSA withdrawal

preparer_flag.flag: FHSA qualifying withdrawal — file Form RC686 with return. Confirm purchase agreement in place before October 1, 2026.

false.message: FHSA funds not used for this purchase. Note that if you now own a home, your FHSA must be closed or transferred to an RRSP within one year of your first qualifying withdrawal deadline or by December 31 of the year you turn 71 — whichever is earlier. Unused funds can be transferred to an RRSP tax-free.

preparer_flag.flag: FHSA not used for purchase — advise on timeline for closing or transferring to RRSP. Funds transferred to RRSP are tax-free but do not restore RRSP room.

screens.title: Did you withdraw from an RRSP under the Home Buyers' Plan (HBP) for this purchase?
screens.instruction: The HBP allows withdrawal of up to $60,000 from an RRSP tax-free for a first home purchase — but unlike the FHSA, HBP withdrawals must be repaid over 15 years.

options.label: Yes — I withdrew from my RRSP under the HBP
I am considering an HBP withdrawal but have not yet done so
No HBP withdrawal

true.message: HBP withdrawal confirmed under s.146.01. You may withdraw up to $60,000 tax-free — but repayment begins in the second year after withdrawal. Annual repayment is 1/15th of the total amount withdrawn. Missed repayments are added to your income for that year. Important: FHSA and HBP can be used simultaneously for the same purchase — if you also withdrew from an FHSA, both withdrawals are valid.

document_checklist_add.document: T4RSP slip — RRSP withdrawal amount under HBP
Form T1036 — Home Buyers' Plan Request to Withdraw Funds from an RRSP — must be filed with return
Purchase agreement — confirms qualifying home purchase

preparer_flag.flag: HBP withdrawal — file Form T1036. Repayment begins second year after withdrawal year. Calculate annual repayment schedule. Confirm FHSA was also used if applicable — both can be used for same purchase.

considering.message: The HBP and FHSA can both be used for the same purchase — maximizing tax-advantaged withdrawals. Key difference: FHSA withdrawals are never repaid, HBP withdrawals must be repaid over 15 years. Your RRSP funds must have been in the plan for at least 90 days before withdrawal. Your preparer can model the optimal combination.

preparer_flag.flag: HBP withdrawal being considered — model FHSA plus HBP combination for maximum tax-free withdrawal. Confirm 90-day rule on RRSP funds under s.146.01.

screens.title: Did you use both an FHSA and the Home Buyers' Plan for this purchase?
screens.instruction: Using both simultaneously maximizes your tax-advantaged withdrawal — up to $40,000 from FHSA (no repayment) plus up to $60,000 from RRSP via HBP (repaid over 15 years).

options.label: Yes — I used both FHSA and HBP
No — I used only one

true.message: Excellent planning — using both FHSA and HBP for the same purchase is fully permitted and maximizes your tax-advantaged position. Your preparer must file both Form RC686 for the FHSA qualifying withdrawal and Form T1036 for the HBP withdrawal. Ensure both slips — T4FHSA and T4RSP — are in hand before filing.

preparer_flag.flag: FHSA and HBP used simultaneously — file both RC686 and T1036. Confirm T4FHSA and T4RSP slips received. Coordinate repayment schedule for HBP portion.

document_checklist_add.document: Both T4FHSA and T4RSP slips — both required when FHSA and HBP used for same purchase

screens.title: Was this the first time you purchased a qualifying home?
screens.instruction: The First Home Buyers' Tax Credit under s.118.05 provides a 15% federal credit on $10,000 — worth $1,500 federally. It is claimed in the year of purchase and is non-refundable.

options.label: Yes — first qualifying home purchase
No — I have purchased a qualifying home before

true.message: First Home Buyers' Tax Credit confirmed under s.118.05. Worth $1,500 federally — a 15% non-refundable credit on $10,000. Claimed on Schedule 1 of your return. No separate application required — your preparer will apply it automatically. Ontario also has a provincial equivalent worth an additional $750.

document_checklist_add.document: Statement of Adjustments from home purchase — confirms purchase date and amount

screens.title: Did you purchase a newly built home or substantially renovated home in 2025?
screens.instruction: Purchasers of newly built or substantially renovated homes may qualify for a GST/HST New Housing Rebate — partially recovering HST paid on the purchase.

options.label: Yes — newly constructed home from a builder
Yes — substantially renovated home
No — resale home (previously occupied)

new_build.message: New construction purchases may qualify for the GST/HST New Housing Rebate under ETA s.254. For homes purchased from a builder, the rebate is often built into the purchase price — confirm with your builder whether the rebate was assigned to them or whether you must claim it directly. Maximum rebate is $6,300 federally for homes under $350,000, with a phase-out to $450,000.

preparer_flag.flag: New construction — nfirm whether GST/HST rebate was assigned to builder or must be claimed directly. Check Ontario New Housing Rebate separately under ETOA.

document_checklist_add.document: Purchase and Sale Agreement showing purchase price and HST treatment — confirms whether rebate assigned to builder

substantial_reno.message: Substantially renovated homes may qualify for the GST/HST New Housing Rebate  the renovation is extensive enough to qualify as new construction under the Excise Tax Act. Your preparer will confirm eligibility based on the scope of renovations.

preparer_flag.flag: Substantial renovation — confirm GST/HST rebate eligibility under ETA s.256. Renovation must meet substantial renovation threshold (90% of interio removed/replaced).

resale.message: Resale homes do not attract GST/HST on the purchase price so the New Housing Rebate does not apply. No action needed on this item.

screens.title: Did you purchase a home in Ontario in 2025?
screens.instruction: Ontario and the City of Toronto offer land transfer tax rebates for first-time buyers. These are administered at closing by your lawyer — not on your tax return — but worth confirming they were applied.

options.label: Yes — Ontario (outside Toronto)
Yes — City of Toronto
No — outside Ontario

ontario_only.message: Ontario first-time buyers are eligible for a land transfer tax rebate of up to $4,000 — applied at closing by your lawyer. Confirm with your lawyer that the rebate was claimed at closing. This is not a T1 item but a closing cost recovery — if it was missed at closing, it may still be claimable through the Ontario Ministry of Finance.

preparer_flag.flag: Ontario Land Transfer Tax Rebate — confirm with lawyer that up to $4,000 rebate was applied at closing. Not a T1 item but verify it was not missed.

toronto.message: City of Toronto buyers pay both Ontario and Toronto land transfer tax. First-time buyers qualify for rebates on both — up to $4,000 Ontario and up to $4,475 Toronto, applied at closing. Confirm both                      were applied by your lawyer. Combined maximum rebate is approximately $8,475.

preparer_flag.flag: Toronto purchase — confirm both Ontario LTT rebate (up to $4,000) and Toronto LTT rebate (up to $4,475) were applied at closing by lawyer.

screens.title: Do you have an existing Home Buyers' Plan balance requiring repayment in 2025?
screens.instruction: If you used the HBP in a prior year, annual repayments are due. Missed repayments are added to your income.

options.label: Yes — I have a prior HBP balance requiring annual repayment
This was my first HBP withdrawal — repayment begins next year
No prior HBP balance

true.message: HBP repayment is due in 2025. The annual repayment is 1/15th of your original HBP withdrawal. Repayments are made as RRSP contributions designated as HBP repayments on Schedule 7. If the repayment is not made, that year's required amount is included in your income. Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment for your outstanding HBP balance.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — shows HBP outstanding balance and required annual repayment
RRSP contribution receipt if HBrepayment was made — designated as HBP repayment on Schedule 7

preparer_flag.flag: HBP repayment due — confirm amount from 2024 NOA. Repayment made as RRSP contribution designated on Schedule 7. If not made, amount added to income.

first_year.message: First year HBP withdrawal — no repayment required in 2025. Repayment begins in the second calendar year after the withdrawal year. Your 2025 Notice of Assessment will show your HBP balance and first required repayment amount.

preparer_flag.flag: First year HBP — no repayment due in 2025. Calculate first epayment year and advise on 2026 repayment planning.

screens.title: Will this home be your principal residence?
screens.instruction: Designating a home as your principal residence shelters any capital gain on sale from tax. You can only have one principal residence per family unit per year.

options.label: Yes — this will be my primary home
Partly — I will also use it for rental income or business
No — this is an investment property

true.message: Principal residen                      designation confirmed. When you sell, the full capital gain is sheltered under s.40(2)(b) — no tax on the gain. No annual election is required while you live there — the designation is made when you sell.

partial.message: Mixed-use property noted. Using part of your principal residence for rental or business may trigger a deemed change of use under s.45(1) — affecting how much of a future gain is sheltered. Your preparer should document the business-use percentage from day one to establish the correct cost allocation.

preparer_flag.flag: Mixed principal residence and rental/business use — document business-use percentage from acquisition date. Consider s.45(1) change of use implications on future sale.

false.message: Investment property noted. Capital gains on future sale are fully taxable — no principal residence exemption. Track your Adjusted Ct Base carefully from acquisition — purchase price plus closing costs form your initial ACB. Capital improvements increase your ACB and reduce future gains.

preparer_flag.flag: Investment property — establish ACB from purchase price plus all closing costs. Track capital improvements to maintain accurate ACB.

document_checklist_add.document: Statement of Adjustments from purchase — all closing costs orm part of ACB for future capital gain calculation

branch_completion_summary.message: First home section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: Statement of Adjustments — key document for purchase price, closing costs, anHST treatment
FHSA contribution receipt and T4FHSA slip if FHSA used
Form RC686 if FHSA qualifying withdrawal was made
T4RSP slip and Form T1036 if HBP withdrawal was made
Purchase agreement — required for both FHSA and HBP qualifying withdrawal confirmation
2024 Notice of Assessment if prior HBP balance exists — confirms repayment amount
Builder's purchase agreement if new construction nfirms HST treatment and rebate status
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we'll cover disability-related deductions and credits.

intro: Disability-related tax items are among the most impactful and most commonly missed in the entire T1 return. The Disability Tax Credit alone can be worth thousands annually — and retroactively up to 10 years. Let's make sure every eligible item is identified.

screens.title: Who has a disability or impairment in your household?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Benefits may flow to the individual directly or to a supporting person depending on the item.

options.label: I have a disability or prolonged impairment
My spouse or common-law partner
My child or grandchild
Another person I support — parent, sibling, or other relative

self.message: Several credits and deductions may apply directly to you. The Disability Tax Credit is the most significant — we'll confirm your status shortly.

spouse.message: Disability benefits for a spouse may flow to your return if your spouse cannot use them fully. We'll confirm DTC status and transfer eligibility.

dependent_child.message: A child with a disability may qualify for the DTC, enhanced child care limits, the RDSP, and the Child Disability Benefit. We'll work through each.

other_dependent.message: Supporting a disabled relative may qualify you for the Caregiver Amount and a DTC transfer if the individual cannot use the credit themselves.

screens.title: Does the person with a disability have an approved Disability Tax Credit (DTC) certificate on file with CRA?
screens.instruction: A DTC certificate is Form T2201 — certified by a medical practitioner and approved by CRA. It is the foundation for most disability benefits. Without it most items in this section are not accessible.

options.label: Yes — Form T2201 has been approved by CRA
Application submitted — awaiting CRA approval
No application has been made
I'm not sure if one exists

approved.message: DTC certificate confirmed. This unlocks the Disability Amount credit under s.118.3, the RDSP, enhanced child care limits, and the Child Disability Benefit if applicable. Bring your approval letter and confirm the certificate period covers 2025.

document_checklist_add.document: CRA DTC approval letter — confirms certificate period and eligible disability amount

applied.message: DTC application pending. If approved before filing, the credit applies to 2025. File your return on time and amend once approved if it comes after filing. Importantly — if approved, the credit can apply retroactively to the start date on the certificate, potentially going back multiple years.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC application pending — file on time and amend return once approved. Retroactive claim possible to certificate start date — up to 10 years.

not_applied.message: A DTC has not been applied for. If the person has a prolonged physical or mental impairment that markedly restricts one or more basic activities of daily living — walking, speaking, hearing, feeding, dressing, mental functions, eliminating — they may qualify. The application is Form T2201 certified by a medical practitioner. If approved, the credit can be claimed retroactively up to 10 years — potentially worth tens of thousands in refunds. This is worth pursuing.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC not applied for — assess eligibility with medical practitioner. Form T2201 required. Retroactive claim up to 10 years if approved — significant refund potential.

unsure.message: Check CRA My Account — DTC certificates are listed there. Alternatively your medical practitioner may have records of a prior application. Given the retroactive claim potential, this is worth confirming urgently.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC status uncertain — check CRA My Account or medical records. If no certificate exists, assess eligibility and apply. Retroactive claim up to 10 years.

screens.title: Can the person with the disability use the full Disability Amount credit on their own return?
screens.instruction: The federal Disability Amount is $9,872 for 2025 — worth approximately $1,481 in tax reduction. If the person with the disability has insufficient income to use the full credit, the unused portion may transfer to a supporting person.

options.label: Yes — they have sufficient income to use the full credit
Partial — they have some income but not enough to use the full credit
No — they have little or no income and cannot use the credit

full_usage.message: Full Disability Amount applied on their own return. No transfer required. The supplement for children under 18 may still apply if applicable — worth an additional $5,758 base amount for 2025.

partial_usage.message: Partial usage noted. The unused portion of the Disability Amount may be transferred to a supporting person under s.118.3(2). Your preparer will calculate the transferable amount after the individual's own return is prepared.

preparer_flag.flag: Partial DTC usage — calculate unused portion transferable to supporting person under s.118.3(2). Requires both returns to be coordinated.

no_usage.message: Full Disability Amount transfer to supporting person confirmed. Under s.118.3(2) the full unused amount transfers to the person who supports the individual — spouse, parent, grandparent, child, sibling, or other qualified relative. Worth up to $1,481 federally in 2025.

preparer_flag.flag: Full DTC transfer to supporting person — confirm relationship qualifies under s.118.3(2) and that supporting person is not already claiming another credit for same individual.

screens.title: Is the person with the disability under 18 years old?
screens.instruction: Children under 18 with an approved DTC qualify for an additional Disability Amount supplement — worth an additional $5,758 in 2025, subject to a child care and attendant care reduction.

options.label: Yes — under 18 at December 31, 2025
No — 18 or older

true.message: Child Disability Supplement confirmed. The supplement is $5,758 for 2025 but is reduced by child care and attendant care expenses claimed for the child above $3,179. Your preparer will calculate the net supplement after applying the reduction.

preparer_flag.flag: Child DTC supplement — reduce by child care and attendant care expenses above $3,179 under s.118.3(1)(c). Net supplement may still be significant.

screens.title: Has the disability or impairment existed for more than one year?
screens.instruction: If an approved DTC certificate covers prior years, you may be able to file adjustments to prior returns and recover significant tax — up to 10 years back.

options.label: Yes — the condition has existed for more than one year
No — the condition first arose in 2025

multiple_years.message: If your DTC certificate covers prior years, you can file T1ADJ adjustments to recover unclaimed Disability Amounts for up to 10 years. This can represent a significant cash recovery — potentially $1,481 per year federally plus provincial amounts. Your preparer will review the certificate start date and file adjustments for all eligible years.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC retroactive claim — review certificate start date and file T1ADJ for all eligible prior years up to 10. Potential refund of ~$1,481/year federal plus provincial.

document_checklist_add.document: DTC approval letter confirming certificate start date — determines how many prior years are eligible for retroactive adjustment

new_2025.message: New disability in 2025 — the credit applies to your 2025 return only. No retroactive adjustments required.

screens.title: Did you or a dependent incur medical expenses in 2025 or in a 12-month period ending in 2025?
screens.instruction: Medical expenses are deductible above a 3% of net income threshold (maximum $2,759 for 2025). The 12-month window rule means you can choose any 12 consecutive months ending in 2025 — not just January to December.

options.label: Yes — significant medical expenses were incurred
Minor expenses only — routine prescriptions and dental checkups
No medical expenses to speak of

true.message: Medical expenses confirmed. The credit is 15% of expenses above the lesser of 3% of net income or $2,759 under s.118.2. Remember the 12-month window rule — choose the 12-month period that maximizes your claim. Your preparer will test multiple periods to find the optimal window.

minor.message: Minor medical expenses likely fall below the 3% net income threshold and may not generate a credit. However if you had one significant expense — a dental procedure, physiotherapy series, vision correction — combined with routine costs, the total may exceed the threshold. Bring all receipts and let your preparer assess.

screens.title: Which types of medical expenses did you incur?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. The ITA medical expense list is broader than most people realize — many items are eligible that taxpayers miss. We are building your document checklist — not asking for amounts.

options.label: Prescription drugs and medications
Dental treatment — including orthodontics
Vision care — glasses, contacts, laser eye surgery
Physiotherapy, chiropractic, massage therapy (if prescribed)
Mental health treatment — psychologist, psychiatrist, licensed therapist
Medical devices — hearing aids, CPAP, wheelchair, prosthetics
Attendant care or nursing home fees
Home modifications for disability — ramps, grab bars, elevator
Travel to obtain medical services not available locally
Fertility treatments — IVF, egg freezing, related procedures
Gluten-free food products — if celiac disease is diagnosed
Other significant medical costs not listed above

prescriptions.message: Prescription drugs are eligible under s.118.2(2)(n) if prescribed by a medical practitioner and recorded by a pharmacist. Your pharmacy can provide an annual summary statement.

document_checklist_add.document: Pharmacy annual summary — all prescription drug costs for the period. Most pharmacies provide this on request.

dental.message: Dental expenses are eligible under s.118.2(2)(a) for services by a dentist or dental hygienist. Cosmetic procedures are generally not eligible. Request year-end statements from all dental providers.

document_checklist_add.document: Dental receipts or year-end statement from dentist — all treatment costs for period

vision.message: Prescription glasses, contact lenses, and laser eye surgery are eligible under s.118.2(2)(j). Non-prescription sunglasses are not eligible. Eye exam fees paid to an optometrist are also eligible.

document_checklist_add.document: Optical receipts — glasses, contacts, surgery, and optometrist exam fees

physiotherapy.message: Physiotherapy by a physiotherapist is eligible under s.118.2(2)(a). Massage therapy is eligible only if performed by a registered massage therapist and prescribed by a medical practitioner — confirm with your preparer. Chiropractic services are eligible.

document_checklist_add.document: Physiotherapy, chiropractic, and massage receipts — note provider designation on each receipt

preparer_flag.flag: Massage therapy — confirm registered designation and prescription requirement under s.118.2(2)(a).

mental_health.message: Mental health treatment is eligible under s.118.2(2)(a) if provided by a medical doctor or psychologist. Licensed counsellors and social workers may also qualify depending on provincial designation — confirm with your preparer. This is an area where eligibility has been expanding.

document_checklist_add.document: Mental health treatment receipts — note provider designation (MD, psychologist, registered counsellor)

preparer_flag.flag: Mental alth expenses — confirm provider qualifies under s.118.2(2)(a). Eligibility varies by practitioner designation and province.

medical_devices.message: Medical devices such as hearing aids, CPAP machines,                      wheelchairs, walkers, and prosthetics are eligible under s.118.2(2) when prescribed by a medical practitioner. Keep the prescription and purchase receipt for each device.

document_checklist_add.document: Medical device receipts plus prescriptions — hearing aids, CPAP, wheelchair, prosthetics and similar

attendant_care.message: Attendant care and nursing home fees are eligible und s.118.2(2)(b) and (b.1). However there is an important interaction with the Disability Supports Deduction under s.64 — some attendant care costs may be better claimed as a deduction rather than a credit. Your preparer will model both options.

preparer_flag.flag: Attendant care — model both medical expense credit under s.118.2(2)(b) and disability supports deduction under s.64. Optimal treatment depends on income level.

document_checklist_add.document: Attendant care or nursing home receipts — provider name, amounts paid, period of care. T4 if care worker is your employee.

home_modifications.message: Eligible disability-related home modifications under s.118.2(2)(l.2) include wheelchair ramps, grab bars, stair lifts, and similar adaptations. The modification must be made to allow a person with a disability to access or be mobile in the home. A DTC certificate strengthens this claim significantly.

document_checklist_add.document: Contractor invoices for home modifications — description of work, disability purpose, and amounts paid

preparer_flag.flag: Home modifications for disability — confirm modification qualifies under s.118.2(2)(l.2). DTC certificate supports claim.

travel_medical.message: Travel expenses to obtain medical services not available within 40km of your home are eligible under s.118.2(2)(g) and (h). This includes mileage, accommodation, and meals for the patient and one companion if required. Keep all receipts and document why local services were not available.

document_checklist_add.document: Travel receipts for medical travel — mileage log, accommodation, meals. Letter from doctor confirming service not available locally.

fertility.message: Fertility treatments including IVF, egg freezing, sperm donation, and related procedures are eligible under s.118.2(2)(a) as of the 2022 budget. This is a relatively new and expanding category — ensure your preparer is aware of recent eligibility expansions.

document_checklist_add.document: Fertility clinic receipts — IVF, egg freezing, all related procedures and medications

gluten_free.message: The incremental cost of gluten-free food products is eligible under s.118.2(2)(r) for individuals with celiac disease confirmed by a medical practitioner. Only the incremental cost above the regular food cost is eligible — not the full purchase price. A letter from your doctor confirming celiac diagnosis is required.

document_checklist_add.document: Gluten-free food receipts plus doctor's letter confirming celiac diagnosis — only incremental cost above regular food is eligible

preparer_flag.flag: Gluten-free food claim — calculate incremental cost only. Doctor's letter required confirming celiac under s.118.2(2)(r).

screens.title: Were your medical expenses concentrated in a specific part of the year — rather than spread evenly throughout 2025?
screens.instruction: The 12-month window rule under s.118.2(1) allows you to choose any 12 consecutive months ending in 2025. If expenses were concentrated — for example, a major procedure in late 2024 followed by recovery costs in early 2025 — a February 2024 to January 2025 window may maximize your claim.

options.label: Yes — expenses were concentrated in a specific period
No — expenses were spread fairly evenly throughout the year
Major expenses crossed calendar years — 2024 into 2025

concentrated.message: 12-month window planning applies. Your preparer will test mtiple 12-month periods ending in 2025 to find the window that maximizes your claim. Bring all receipts with dates — the dates are what allow the optimal window to be selected.

preparer_flag.flag: Medical expenses concentrated — test multiple 12-month windows ending in 2025 to maximize claim under s.118.2(1). All receipts must be dated.

cross_year.message: Cross-year expenses are thedeal scenario for the 12-month window rule. A window starting in mid-2024 and ending in mid-2025 may capture the highest total. Bring all receipts from both calendar years — your preparer                      will determine the optimal 12-month period.

preparer_flag.flag: Cross-year medical expenses — bring receipts from both 2024 and 2025. Test 12-month windows spanning both years for maximum claim.

document_checklist_add.document: Medical receipts from both 2024 and 2025 — all dated. 12-month window may span both years.

screens.title: Do you wanto combine your medical expenses with your spouse's?
screens.instruction: Medical expenses for a spouse or dependents can be claimed by either spouse — and it is always more efficient to claim all family medical expensesn the lower-income spouse's return, as the 3% threshold is lower.

options.label: Yes — both spouses had medical expenses
No — only one spouse had expenses

true.message: Combining family medical expenses on the lower-income spouse's return is almost always more efficient — the 3% net income threshold is lower, meaning more expenses exceed the floor. Your preparer will confirm the optimal allocation. Both spouses' receipts should be brought to the same appointment.

preparer_flag.flag: Combined family medical expenses — claim on lower-income spouse return for maximum credit. Confirm optimal allocation after both net incomes are calculated.

screens.title: Does the person with the disability have a Registered Disability Savings Plan (RDSP)?
screens.instruction: An RDSP is a long-term savings plan for individuals with an approved DTC certificate. The government contributes through the Canada Disability Savings Grant and Canada Disability Savings Bond — potentially worth up to $3,500 and $1,000 annually respectively.

options.label: Yes — an RDSP exists and contributions were made in 2025
Yes — an RDSP exists but no contribution in 2025
No RDSP has been opened

existing.message: RDSP contributions confirmed. Contributions are not tax deductible — unlike RRSP. However the Canada Disability Savings Grant (CDSG) matches contributions at 100% to 300% depending on family income, up to $3,500 annually and $70,000 lifetime. The Canada Disability Savings Bond (CDSB) provides up to $1,000 annually for low-income beneficiaries — no contribution required to receive the Bond.

document_checklist_add.document: RDSP contribution receipt and account statement — confirms contributions and government grant amounts

existing_no_contribution.message: No contribution made in 2025. Note that unused CDSG and CDSB room carries forward — contributing in future years can catch up on missed grants going back 10 years. The Canada Disability Savings Bond is paid automatically without any contribution — confirm the bond was received if income was below threshold.

preparer_flag.flag: RDSP with no 2025 contribution — confirm CDSB was received if income eligible. Advise on catch-up contribution strategy for prior unused grant room.

no_rdsp.message: An RDSP has not been opened. With an approved DTC certificate, opening an RDSP is strongly advisable — the government grants and bonds can be claimed retroactively for the 10 years prior to opening (subject to the DTC certificate period). For a low-income beneficiary, opening an RDSP with a $1,500 contribution could generate $3,500 in government grants in the first year alone. Your preparer should prioritize this discussion.

preparer_flag.flag: No RDSP opened — CRITICAL for DTC-approved individual. Government grants retroactive 10 years. Opening RDSP immediately maximizes catch-up grants. Advise urgently.

screens.title: Did the person with the disability pay for any of the following supports to enable them to work, carry on a business, or attend school?
screens.instruction: The Disability Supports Deduction under s.64 is a deduction — not a cred — and applies to a specific list of supports that enable the individual to earn income or attend school. It may overlap with the medical expense credit and your preparer will determine the optimal treatment.

options.label: Yes — sign language interpreter, Braille materials, note-taking services, voice recognition software, or similar supports
No supports of this nature

true.message: Disability supports deduction confirmed under s.64. Eligible items include sign language interpretation, Braille materials, note-taking services, tutoring for individuals with learning disabilities, voice recognition software, and other prescribed supports. The deduction is limited to earned income in the year. Your preparer will determine whether the deduction or medical expense credit is more beneficial for each item.

document_checklist_add.document: Disability support receipts — sign language, Braille, note-taking, voice software, tutoring

preparer_flag.flag: Disability supports — compare s.64 deduction vs s.118.2 medical expense credit for each item. Deduction more valuable at high marginal rates.

screens.title: Does the person with the disability have employment or self-employment income?
screens.instruction: Individuals with an approved DTC who have earned income may qualify for the Disability Supplement of the Canada Workers Benefit — worth up to $784 additionally in 2025.

options.label: Yes — employment or self-employment income present
No earned income

true.message: Canada Workers Benefit Disability Supplement may apply under s.122.7. This is a refundable credit — worth up to $784 additionally for DTC-eligible individuals with earned income. Phase-out begins around $33,018 net income. Your preparer will calculate eligibility automatically from the return.

preparer_flag.flag: CWB Disability Supplement — confirm DTC certificate active and calculate eligibility under s.122.7. Refundable credit up to $784.

screens.title: Are you receiving the Child Disability Benefit (CDB) for a child with a disability?
screens.instruction: The Child Disability Benefit is a tax-free monthly supplement to the Canada Child Benefit for families with a child under 18 who has an approved DTC certificate. Worth up to $3,322 annually for 2025.

options.label: Yes — CDB is being received
No — not yet applied or not receiving
I'm not sure if I'm receiving it

true.message: Child Disability Benefit confirmed. It is not taxable income — no T1 reporting required. However it is recalculated each July based on prior year net income — filing on time is important to avoid                    interruption.

preparer_flag.flag: CDB recipient — file return on time to avoid interruption. CDB recalculated July based on prior year income.

false.message: With an approved DTC certificate for ahild under 18, the Child Disability Benefit is automatic through the CCB application. If you are receiving CCB and have a DTC approval, CRA should have added the CDB automatically. Check CRA My Account to confirm it is being paid. If not, contact CRA directly.

preparer_flag.flag: CDB not being received — DTC approved for child under 18. Confirm CDB is being paid through CCB account. If not, contact CRA to trigger payment. Worth up to $3,322 annually.

unsure.message: Check CRA My Account under Canada Child Benefit — the CDB appears as a separate line. If you have an approved DTC for a child under 18 and are not receiving CDB, contact CRA immediately.

preparer_flag.flag: CDB status uncertain — check CRA My Account and confirm payment. DTC-approved child under8 should be receiving CDB.

screens.title: Does the person with the disability live in Ontario and pay rent or property tax?
screens.instruction: The Ontario Trillium Benefit includes an energy component that has a higher rate for individuals with disabilities living on a reserve or in a care facility.

options.label: Yes — Ontario resident paying rent or property tax
No

true.message: Ontario Trillium Benefit confirmed as relevant.                      The OTB application on Form ON-BEN is filed with your Ontario return. Your preparer will confirm whether the disability affects any component of the OTB calculation.

document_checklist_add.document: Rent receipts or property tax bills — required for OTB application on Form ON-BEN

branch_completion_summary.message: Disability section complete. ere is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: CRA DTC approval letter — confirms certificate period and eligible amount. Check start date for retroactive claim potential.
All medical receipts with dates — 12-month window optimization requires dated receipts. Pharmacy annual summary is most efficient.
Attendant care receipts or nursing home statement bring for both deduction and credit comparison.
RDSP account statement and contribution receipts if applicable.
Disability support receipts — sign language, Braille, voice software, tutoring.
Medical device prescriptions and purchase receipts.
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we'll cover age milestones that affect your 2025 tax return.

intro: Certain ages trigger specific tax rules — some opening new benefits, others closing existing ones. Let's make sure the right items are activated or closed off for your situation.

screens.title: Which age milestone applies to you in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Each has different tax implications.

options.label: I turned 18 in 2025
I turned 65 in 2025
I turned 71 in 2025

turned_18.message: Turning 18 in 2025 opens several accounts and credits for the first time — TFSA eligibility being the most significant. We'll cover each item.

turned_65.message: Turning 65 unlocks the Age Amount credit, Pension Income Credit eligibility, and pension income splitting rights. These apply from January 1 of the year you turn 65 — not just from your birthday.

turned_71.message: Turning 71 triggers the mandatory RRSP conversion deadline — December 31 of this year. This is the most time-sensitive item in this branch. We'll confirm the status immediately.

screens.title: Have you opened a Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) since turning 18?
screens.instruction: Canadians become eligible for a TFSA at age 18. Contribution room accumulates from the year you turn 18 — even if you have not yet opened an account.

options.label: Yes — opened and contributed in 2025
Opened but have not yet contributed
Not yet opened

opened_contributed.message: TFSA confirmed. Your 2025 contribution room is $7,000 — the annual limit for 2025. This is the first year of room accumulation since you turned 18 this year. Ensure contributions do not exceed $7,000 — a 1% per month penalty applies on overcontributions.

preparer_flag.flag: First year TFSA — confirm contribution does not exceed $7,000 room for 2025. Overcontribution penalty is 1% per month.

opened_not_contributed.message: TFSA opened but no contribution yet. Your 2025 room is $7,000 and can be used any time before December 31, 2025. Unused room carries forward to 2026 — no deadline to contribute in 2025.

not_opened.message: A TFSA is worth opening now. Investment returns inside a TFSA are completely tax-free — unlike a regular savings account. Your 2025 room is $7,000 and carries forward if unused. Opening takes minutes through any financial institution.

preparer_flag.flag: Age 18 — advise on opening TFSA to begin room accumulation. 2025 room is $7,000.

screens.title: Is this the first year you are filing your own tax return?

options.label: Yes — first year filing my own return
No — I have filed previously as a minor

true.message: First-year filer noted. Several items are worth establishing from your first return: RRSP contribution room begins accumulating from earned income reported, GST/HST Credit eligibility begins at 19 (or when you move out of your parents' home), and any tuition paid in 2025 generates credits that carry forward indefinitely. Filing even with no tax owing establishes your tax history with CRA.

preparer_flag.flag: First year filer — establish RRSP earned income base, confirm GST/HST Credit eligibility at 19, carry forward any tuition credits. Filing builds CRA tax history.

false.message: Prior filing history noted. Your existing RRSP room and tuition carryforwards from prior returns carry forward automatically. Bring your most recent Notice of Assessment.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP room and any tuition credit carryforward balance

screens.title: Did your parents transfer property or funds to you that were generating investment income?
screens.instruction: Attribution rules that taxed investment income in your parents' hands end in the year you turn 18. From 2025 onward income on transferred property is taxed in your hands — not your parents'.

options.label: Yes — I have investments or accounts funded by my parents
No

true.message: Attribution rules under s.74.1(2) end in the year you turn 18. From 2025 income on transferred property is taxed in your hands at your marginal rate — not your parents'. Your parents' preparer should stop reporting this income on their return from 2025 onward. Coordinate with your parents to ensure the income is not double-reported.

preparer_flag.flag: Attribution rules end at 18 — income on funds transferred by parents now taxed in child's hands under s.74.1(2). Coordinate with parents' preparer to avoid double reporting.

screens.title: Did you receive any eligible pension income in 2025 — the year you turned 65?
screens.instruction: Turning 65 opens the Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) for RRSP withdrawals converted to annuity or RRIF — which do not qualify for this credit before age 65. This is a planning opportunity worth acting on immediately.

options.label: Yes — employer defined benefit or defined contribution pension
Yes — RRIF or annuity income
RRSP withdrawals only — not yet converted to RRIF
No pension income yet — still working

employer_pension.message: Employer pension income at 65 qualifies for the Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) — worth up to $300 federally on the first $2,000. Also now eligible for pension income splitting under s.60.03 if you have a spouse.

rrif_annuity.message: RRIF and annuity income qualifies for the Pension Income Credit at age 65 under s.118(3). Before 65, RRIF income does not qualify for this credit — so turning 65 materially improves the tax treatment of your RRIF withdrawals.

rrsp_only.message: RRSP withdrawals do not qualify for the Pension Income Credit at any age — only RRIF income qualifies after 65. If you are making RRSP withdrawals, converting a portion to a RRIF now allows you to access the Pension Income Credit and pension splitting on that income. This is a planning discussion worth having with your preparer this year.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP withdrawals at 65 — consider partial RRIF conversion to access Pension Income Credit under s.118(3) and pension splitting. RRSP withdrawals do not qualify.

none.message: Age Amount credit activates at 65 regardless of pension income — worth up to $1,259 federally subject to income phase-out. Pension Income Credit requires eligible pension income — you may access this by making a small RRIF withdrawal of at least $2,000 to maximize the credit for the minimum tax cost.

preparer_flag.flag: Turned 65 with no pension income — advise on small RRIF conversion of at least $2,000 to access Pension Income Credit under s.118(3). Credit worth $300 on minimum $2,000 withdrawal.

screens.title: Have you applied for Old Age Security?

options.label: Yes — OAS is being received or application is in progress
I am deferring OAS past 65 to increase the benefit
No — I have not applied yet

true.message: OAS application confirmed. OAS payments begin the month after you apply — they are not retroactive beyond 11 months. Apply promptly if not already receiving payments.

deferring.message: OAS deferral is a legitimate strategy — each month of deferral past 65 increases your monthly benefit by 0.6%, up to a maximum 36% increase at age 70. This is most valuable if you expect to live past the break-even age of approximately 74-75 years. Your preparer can model the break-even point.

preparer_flag.flag: OAS deferral in progress — confirm deferral strategy and model break-even age vs immediate receipt. Maximum deferral to 70 increases benefit by 36%.

false.message: OAS applications should be submitted to Service Canada approximately 6 months before you want payments to begin. Payments are not retroactive beyond 11 months — if you have been eligible since turning 65 and have not applied, apply immediately to preserve up to 11 months of retroactive entitlement.

preparer_flag.flag: OAS not applied — apply to Service Canada immediately. Retroactive only 11 months — delay costs real money.

screens.title: Have you applied for Canada Pension Plan (CPP) benefits?

options.label: Yes — CPP is being received or applied for
I started CPP early — before 65
I am deferring CPP past 65
No — not yet applied

true.message: CPP application confirmed. CPP is taxable income under s.56(1)(a) and reported on a T4A(P) slip. At 65 you also gain the right to share CPP with a spouse through Service Canada for income splitting purposes.

early.message: Early CPP noted. Taking CPP before 65 reduces the benefit by 0.6% per month before 65 — a maximum 36% reduction if taken at 60. No retroactive adjustment is available once payments begin — the election is permanent.

deferring.message: CPP deferral past 65 increases the benefit by 0.7% per month — a maximum 42% increase at age 70. This is one of the best guaranteed return strategies available. Your preparer can model the break-even age given your health and income situation.

preparer_flag.flag: CPP deferral — model break-even age. Maximum 42% increase at 70. Consider OAS clawback implications of higher future CPP income.

false.message: CPP can be taken between age 60 and 70. Taking it now at 65 gives the standard rate — no increase or decrease. Deferring past 65 increases the benefit by 0.7% per month. Your preparer can model the optimal timing based on your other income sources.

preparer_flag.flag: CPP not yet applied — model optimal start date between 65 and 70. Consider OAS clawback, tax bracket, and longevity in the analysis.

screens.title: Are you maximizing your TFSA contributions now that you are 65?
screens.instruction: At 65 with pension income, the TFSA becomes even more valuable — withdrawals do not affect OAS clawback thresholds or income-tested benefits, unlike RRIF withdrawals.

options.label: Yes — maximizing TFSA contributions
Partial contributions but not maximizing
No — not contributing to TFSA

true.message: TFSA maximization confirmed. At 65+ this is particularly valuable — TFSA withdrawals are not included in net income and therefore do not trigger OAS clawback, Age Amount phase-out, or GIS reduction. Shifting investments from non-registered accounts to TFSA progressively is an excellent long-term strategy.

false.message: At 65 the TFSA is worth prioritizing over other non-registered savings — returns are tax-free and withdrawals don't affect OAS or other income-tested benefits. Your cumulative room to 2025 is $102,000 if you have never contributed. Your preparer can confirm your available room from CRA My Account.

preparer_flag.flag: Age 65 — TFSA optimization important for OAS clawback management. Confirm available room and advise on shifting non-registered savings to TFSA.

screens.title: Have you converted your RRSP to a RRIF or annuity?
screens.instruction: This is mandatory by December 31 of the year you turn 71 — regardless of what date in 2025 your birthday falls. Failure to convert results in the entire RRSP balance being included in your 2026 income.

options.label: Yes — converted to RRIF or annuity
Conversion is in progress with my financial institution
No — not yet converted

true.message: RRSP conversion confirmed. Bring your conversion confirmation from your financial institution showing the conversion was completed before December 31, 2025.

document_checklist_add.document: RRIF conversion confirmation from financial institution — date of conversion must be before December 31, 2025

in_progress.message: Conversion in progress — ensure your financial institution completes this before December 31, 2025. The deadline is absolute — there are no extensions. Confirm with them today that the conversion will be completed in time.

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: RRSP conversion in progress — confirm with financial institution it will complete before December 31, 2025. No extensions available.

false.message: CRITICAL — your RRSP must be convert                      to a RRIF or annuity before December 31, 2025. Contact your financial institution today. If you are reading this after December 31, 2025 without having converted, contact your preparer immediately — the full RRSP balance will be included in your 2026 income and there may be limited remediation options.

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: RRSP not converted at age 71. Contact financial institution immediately. Deadline is December 31, 2025 — absolute. Failure means full RRSP balance taxable in 2026.

screens.title: Did you make a final RRSP conibution in 2025 before converting?
screens.instruction: The year you turn 71 is the last year you can contribute to your own RRSP. Contributions made up to March 1, 2026 can be deducted on your 2025 return — but the RRSP must still be converted by December 31, 2025.

options.label: Yes — final contribution made
I still have RRSPm and am considering a final contribution
No final contribution

true.message: Final RRSP contribution confirmed. Bring your contribution receipt — deductible on your 2025 retu if made by March 1, 2026.

document_checklist_add.document: Final RRSP contribution receipt — deductible on 2025 return if made by Mar 1, 2026

considering.message: A final RRSP contribution this year uses any remaining room before it is permanently lost. Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment for your remaining room. Contributions are deductible on your 2025 return if made by March 1, 2026 — even after converting to a RRIF. However note that contributing to your own RRSP after the plan is convted to a RRIF is not possible — but you may still contribute to a spousal RRSP if your spouse is under 72.

preparer_flag.flag: Final RRSP contribution being considered — confirm remaining room from 2024 N Spousal RRSP contribution still possible if spouse under 72. Contribution deadline March 1, 2026.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms remaining RRSP contribution room for final contribution decision

screens.title: Does your spouse have an RRSP and are they under 72?
screens.instruction: Even after converting your own RRSP to a RRIF at 71, you may continue contributing to a spousal RRSP using your contribution room — as long as your spouse has not yet turned 72.

options.label: Yes — spouse has an RRSP and is under 72
No — spouse is 72 or older or has no RRSP

true.message: Spousal RRSP contributions are still available using your contribution room even after your own RRSP has been converted to a RRIF. This is a valuable income splitting opportunity — your spouse will eventually withdraw from their RRSP or RRIF at their lower marginal rate. Contributions can be made until your spouse turns 71. The two-year attribution ru still applies on withdrawals.

preparer_flag.flag: Spousal RRSP still available — contribute using remaining room even after own RRSP convert. Income splitting benefit. Two-year attribution applies on withdrawals.

screens.title: Have you and your financial advisor discussed your RRIF withdrawal strategy?

options.label: Yes — strategy is in place
No — I have not discussed this yet
