true.message: RRIF strategy confirmed. Bring your withdrawal schedule to your preparer — they will confirm the minimum withdrawal was met and apply pension income splitting if applicable.

preparer_flag.flag: RRIF withdrawal strategy in place â                   confirm minimum met and model pension splitting on RRIF income with spouse.

false.message: RRIF withdrawal strategy is worth establishing now. Key decisions: take only the minimum each year to preserve tax-deferred growth, or withdraw more at lower rates to reduce future mandatory minimums and estate tax exposure. Using your spouse's age for the minimum calculation if they are younger reduces mandatory withdrawals. Your preparer and financial advisor should coordinate on this.

preparer_flag.flag: No RRIF strategy in place — advise on minimum vs accelerated withdrawal plning. Consider using younger spouse's age for minimum calculation. Coordinate with financial advisor.

branch_completion_summary.message: Age milestones section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: RRIF conversion confirmation from financial institution — critical if you turned 71. Must show conversion before December 31, 2025.
2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP room for final contribution if turning 71.
T4A(P) and T4A(OAS) slips if CPP or OAS was applied for.
RRIF account statement showing January 1 balance — for minimum withdrawal calculation.
Most reOA — confirms TFSA room for age 18 milestone.
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we'll cover registered savings plans — RRSP, TFSA, and FHSA contributions for 2025.

intro: Registered savings plans are among the most powerful tax tools available. Let's make sure your RRSP, TFSA, and FHSA positions are optimized for 2025 — and that any repayment obligations are confirmed.

screens.title: Did you contribute to an RRSP in 2025 or plan to by March 1, 2026?
screens.instruction: RRSP contributions made between January 1, 2025 and March 1, 2026 can be deducted on your 2025 return. The contribution deadline is March 1, 2026 — not April 30.

options.label: Yes — I contributed in 2025
I plan to contribute before March 1, 2026
I have no contribution room or have already maximized
No — I did not and do not plan to

contributed.message: RRSP contribution confirmed. Your financial institution will issue a contribution receipt — two receipts if you contributed in both calendar periods (March 2 to December 31, 2025 and January 1 to March 1, 2026). Bring both receipts as they cover different periods.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP contribution receipts — all receipts for March 2, 2025 to March 1, 2026 period. Two receipts if contributions in both calendar years.

plan_to_contribute.message: RRSP contribution deadline is March 1, 2026 — act before this date. Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment for your available room. Contributions made in January or February 2026 can be deducted on your 2025 return if designated for 2025. Your preparer will confirm the optimal amount.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP contribution planned — deadline March 1, 2026. Confirm room from 2024 NOA and optimal contribution amount before appointment.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms available RRSP contribution room for 2025

no_room.message: No RRSP room noted. Confirm your room on your 2024 Notice of Assessment — even a small contribution room balance from prior years may be available.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP room confirmed as zero or maximized — verify from 2024 NOA.

did_not.message: No RRSP contribution for 2025. Unused room carries forward indefinitely — it is not lost. However the tax-deferred compounding opportunity for 2025 is foregone. Your preparer can confirm your accumulated room for future planning.

screens.title: Do you know approximately how much RRSP contribution room you have for 2025?
screens.instruction: Your 2025 RRSP room is shown on your 2024 Notice of Assessment. It includes unused room from prior years plus 18% of your 2024 earned income up to $32,490.

options.label: Yes — I contributed the full amount available
Yes — I know my room but contributed only a portion
No — I contributed an amount but am not sure of my total room

know_maximized.message: RRSP fully maximized for 2025. No further action required on contribution room.

know_partial.message: Partial RRSP contribution noted. Unused room carries forward — it is not lost. However consider whether additional contribution before March 1, 2026 is beneficial given your current income level.

preparer_flag.flag: Partial RRSP contribution — confirm unused room carries forward and advise on additional contribution before March 1, 2026 deadline.

dont_know.message: Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment for your total available room. If you over-contributed by more than $2,000 above your room, a 1% per month penalty applies. Your preparer will verify your room and confirm no overcontribution.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP room uncertain — verify from 2024 NOA. Confirm no overcontribution above $2,000 permitted buffer. Penalty is 1% per month on excess.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP contribution room and overcontribution risk

screens.title: Do you expect your income to be significantly higher in 2026 than in 2025?
screens.instruction: You can contribute to an RRSP now but choose to claim the deduction in a future year when your marginal rate is higher. The contribution starts growing tax-free immediately while the deduction waits for a more valuable year.

options.label: Yes — I expect significantly higher income in 2026 or later
No — my income is stable or 2025 is a higher-income year
Not sure

true.message: RRSP deduction timing opportunity identified. You can contribute to your RRSP now and carry forward the deduction to claim in 2026 or later when your marginal rate is higher. The contribution immediately begins growing tax-free inside the RRSP. This is a planning discussion for your preparer — the decision depends on the rate difference and how soon income increases.

checklist_update.planning_note: Consider deferring deduction to 2026 if marginal rate will be higher

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP deduction timing opportunity — higher income expected in 2026. Advise on contributing now and deferring deduction claim to maximize marginal rate benefit.

unsure.message: Worth discussing with your preparer. If a significant income event is likely in the next few years — promotion, business expansion, inheritance — deferring the RRSP deduction could be more valuable than claiming it now.

preparer_flag.flag: Discuss RRSP deduction timing — deferred deduction more valuable if higher marginal rate expected in future years.

screens.title: Which of the following best describes your earned income in 2025?
screens.instruction: RRSP room for 2026 is 18% of your 2025 earned income up to $32,490. Earned income includes employment, business income, rental income, and spousal support received — but not investment income or pension.

options.label: Above $180,500 — I will hit the maximum $32,490 room for 2026
$30,000 – $180,500 — room will be 18% of earned income
Under $30,000 — limited room accumulating
No earned income — no new room for 2026

high.message: Maximum RRSP room of $32,490 will be added to your 2026 limit. This will appear on your 2025 Notice of Assessment. If you have accumulated unused room from prior years, it adds to this amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Maximum RRSP room accruing for 2026 — confirm 2025 NOA shows $32,490 new room plus any carryforward.

low.message: Limited RRSP room accumulating in 2026. For lower-income years, TFSA contributions may be more efficient than RRSP — the RRSP deduction saves less tax at lower marginal rates while TFSA returns are always completely tax-free. Worth discussing with your preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: Low earned income year — compare RRSP vs TFSA efficiency. TFSA may be superior at low marginal rates. Alternatively contribute to RRSP but defer deduction to higher-income year.

none.message: No earned income means no new RRSP room for 2026. However unused room from prior years carries forward indefinitely — it is not lost. Your NOA will confirm your total available room.

screens.title: Are you aware of your total RRSP contributions across all plans — including any spousal RRSP?
screens.instruction: RRSP overcontributions above $2,000 attract a 1% per month penalty tax under s.204.1(2.1). This applies to your total contributions across all RRSPs including spousal plans.

options.label: Yes — I track my total contributions and am within my room
No — I am not certain of my total position

false.message: RRSP overcontribution check flagged. Check CRA My Account — it shows your total RRSP room and contributions in real time. A $2,000 buffer is permitted above your room limit without penalty — but anything above that is penalized at 1% per month. Your preparer will verify your position from all contribution receipts.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP overcontribution risk — verify total contributions across all plans including spousal. Confirm against room from 2024 NOA. Penalty is 1% per month on excess above $2,000 buffer.

screens.title: Did you contribute to a TFSA in 2025?
screens.instruction: The 2025 annual TFSA limit is $7,000. Total cumulative room since 2009 is $102,000 for those who have been 18 and Canadian residents since 2009. Withdrawals made in 2024 restore room in 2025.

options.label: Yes — contributed to my TFSA
Yes — contributed to my spouse's TFSA as well
I withdrew funds in 2024 and recontributed in 2025
No TFSA contributions in 2025

contributed.message: TFSA contribution confirmed. Ensure total 2025 contributions do not exceed your available room — the $7,000 annual limit plus any withdrawals made in 2024 that restored room. CRA My Account shows your current room in real time.

contributed_spouse.message: Spousal TFSA contribution noted. Contributing to your spouse's TFSA uses their room — not yours. Ensure your spouse's total contributions do not exceed their available room. Unlike a spousal RRSP, there are no attribution rules on TFSA withdrawals.

preparer_flag.flag: Spousal TFSA contribution — confirm uses spouse's room. No attribution on withdrawals unlike spousal RRSP.

withdrew_recontributed.message: TFSA withdrawal and recontribution noted. Withdrawals made in 2024 restore room on January 1, 2025 — not immediately. If you withdrew and recontributed in the same calendar year without sufficient room, an overcontribution penalty may apply. Confirm with CRA My Account that your room covered the recontribution.

preparer_flag.flag: TFSA withdrawal and recontribution — confirm 2024 withdrawals restored room January 1, 2025 before recontribution. Same-year withdrawal and recontribution is a common overcontribution error.

screens.title: Have you ever exceeded your TFSA contribution room?
screens.instruction: TFSA overcontributions are common — especially when taxpayers withdraw and recontribute in the same calendar year. CRA charges 1% per month on the excess and issues assessment letters.

options.label: Yes — I received a letter from CRA about an overcontribution
Possibly — I am not certain of my room at all times
No — I have never overcontributed

true.message: CRA TFSA overcontribution letter noted. Bring the letter to your preparer — the penalty tax is reported on Form RC243 and is due June 30 for the prior year. The excess contribution must be withdrawn immediately to stop the penalty accruing.

document_checklist_add.document: CRA TFSA overcontribution letter — bring to preparer. Form RC243 required.

preparer_flag.flag: TFSA overcontribution penalty outstanding — file Form RC243, confirm excess withdrawn, calculate penalty due June 30.

possible.message: TFSA room uncertainty flagged. Check CRA My Account — it shows your room in real time including all contributions and withdrawals reported by financial institutions. Note: there is sometimes a delay in reporting — CRA's view of your room may lag by several months.

preparer_flag.flag: TFSA room uncertain — verify from CRA My Account. Note reporting lag — confirm all 2025 contributions with financial institution directly.

screens.title: Were you a Canadian resident for all of 2025?
screens.instruction: TFSA contribution room only accumulates during years when you are a Canadian resident and 18 or older. If you arrived in Canada partway through the year, your room is prorated.

options.label: Yes — full year Canadian resident
No — I became a Canadian resident part-way through 2025

false.message: TFSA room does not accumulate for the period before you became a Canadian resident. You receive the full $7,000 for 2025 regardless of when you arrived — TFSA room is not prorated within the year, but years before becoming a resident do not generate room. Your cumulative room starts from your arrival year.

preparer_flag.flag: Newcomer TFSA — confirm room accumulates from year of Canadian residency only. Full $7,000 for arrival year regardless of arrival month.

screens.title: Did you contribute to an FHSA in 2025?
screens.instruction: If you have not yet purchased a home and are eligible, the FHSA provides both a tax deduction on contributions and tax-free growth — the most powerful combination available for first-time buyers.

options.label: Yes — contributed to FHSA in 2025
I oned an FHSA but have not yet contributed
I do not have an FHSA and am not a first-time buyer
I am a first-time buyer but have not opened an FHSA yet

contributed.message: FHSA contribution confirmed. Contributions are deductible under s.146.6 up to $8,000 for 2025 with a $40,000 lifetime limit. Unlike RRSP, unused FHSA room carries forward only one year — aximum $8,000 carry-forward. Your financial institution will issue a contribution receipt.

document_checklist_add.document: FHSA contribution receipt — 2025 contribution amount from financial institution

opened_only.message: FHSA opened but no contribution. Contributions made up to March 1, 2026 are deductible on your 2025 return — same deadline as RRSP. Opening the account also activates your contribution room — contributing $8,000 now plus another $8,000 after January 1, 2026 before March 1 maximizes your 2025 deduction.

preparerlag.flag: FHSA opened with no contribution — deadline March 1, 2026 for 2025 deduction. Consider maximizing $8,000 before deadline.

eligible_not_opened.message: You are eligible for an FHSA but have not opened one. Opening and contributing before March 1, 2026 generates a 2025 deduction. The account must be opened while you are still a first-time buyer — if you are about to purchase, open the count before closing. Even a $1 contribution opens the room.

preparer_flag.flag: FHSA eligible but not opened — advise on opening immediately. Must be opened before home purchase closes. Contribution before Mar 1, 2026 deductible on 2025 return.

screens.title: Do you have an outstanding Home Buyers' Plan balance requiring repayment in 2025?
screens.instruction: HBP annual repayments are made as RRSP contributions designated on Schedule 7. Missed repayments are added to your income for the year.

options.label: Yes — I made my required repayment in 2025
Yes — but I did not make the repayment
I have an HBP balance but am not sure of the rment amount

repaid.message: HBP repayment confirmed. Bring your RRSP contribution receipt designated as HBP repayment — and confirm it was designated on Schedule 7, not claimed as a regular RRSP deduction.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP contribution receipt designated as HBP repayment — confirm Schedule 7 designation, not regular RRSP deduction

not_repaid.message: Missed HBP repayment — the required annual amount is added to your 2025 income under s.146.01(4). Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment for the repayment amount. You can still make the repayment as an RRSP contribution before March 1, 2026 and have it applied to 2025 — contact your preparer immediately.

preparer_flag.flag: HBP repayment missed — required amount added to 2025 income. RRSP contribution by March 1, 2026 designated as HBP repayment can offset income inclusion. Act immediately.

unsure.message: HBP repayment amount is shown on your 2024 Notice of Assessment — check the last page. Your preparer will also confirm the amount and whether a repayment was made.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — shows HBP balance and required annual repayment amount

screens.title: Do you have an outstanding Lifelong Learning Plan balance requiring repaynt in 2025?
screens.instruction: LLP annual repayments are made as RRSP contributions. Missed repayments are added to your income for the year.

options.label: Yes — repayment made in 2025
Yes — repayment not made
I have an LLP balance but am unsure of the amount

repaid.message: LLP repayment confirmed. Bring your RRSP contribution receipt designated as LLP repayment on Schedule 7.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP contribution receipt designated as LLP repayment — Schedule 7 designation

not_repaid.message: Missed LLP repayment — the required annual amount is added to your 2025 income under s.146.02(4). An RRSP contribution designated as LLP repayment by March 1, 2026 can offset this. Contact your preparer immediately.

preparer_flag.flag: LLP repayment missed — required amount added to 2025 income under s.146.02(4). RRScontribution by March 1, 2026 as LLP repayment can offset. Act immediately.

unsure.message: LLP balance and repayment amount shown on your most recent Notice of Assessment. Check the carryforward section on the last page.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — shows LLP balance and required annual repayment

screens.title: Are you unsure whether to prioritize RRSP or TFSA contributions with available savings?
screens.instruction: This is one of the most common planning questions. The answer depends on your current and expected future marginal tax rates.

options.label: Yes — I would like guidance on this
No — I have a clear strategy

true.message: RRSP vs TFSA decision framework: RRSP is better when your current marginal rate is higher than your expected rate in retirement — the deduction saves more tax now than the withdrawal will cost later. TFSA is better when your current rate is lower than expected fute rate — or when you want flexibility, since TFSA withdrawals do not affect OAS clawback, GIS, or other income-tested benefits. For most people in their working years, the answer is both âimize TFSA first at lower incomes, RRSP first at higher incomes.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP vs TFSA optimization discussion requested — model based on current marginal rate, expected retirement rate, OAS clawback sensitivity, and flexibility needs.

screens.title: Do you have significant unused RRSP contribution room from prior years?
screens.instruction: Unused RRSP room carries forward indefinitely. If you have accumulated large unused room over many years, a catch-up strategy may be worth considering.

options.label: Yes — I have substantial unused room accumulated over many years
Some room — a few years of unused contributions
Minimal or no unused room — I contribute regularly

large_room.message: Large RRSP room carryforward noted. This represents a significant tax planning opportunity — particularly if you have a high-income year, receive a lump sum (bonus, inheritance, property sale), or plan to wind down a business. A catch-up contribution in a high-income year can save significant tax at peak marginal rates. Your preparer can confirm the total room from your NOA.

preparer_flag.flag: Large RRSP carryforward room — advise on catch-up strategy. Optimal to deploy in high-income year for maximum marginal rate saving.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assement — confirms total accumulated RRSP room carryforward

branch_completion_summary.message: Savings and registered plans section complete. Here is what we've identified:
highlighted_documents: RRSP contrution receipts — all for March 2, 2025 to March 1, 2026 period. Two receipts if contributions in both calendar years.
2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP room, TFSA room, HBP balance, and LLP balance. Single most important document for this branch.
FHSA contribution receipt if applicable.
RRSP contribution receipt designated as HBP or LLP repayment — confirm Schedule 7 desig.
TFSA overcontribution letter from CRA if received.
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: we'll do a final sweep of deductions to make sure nothing has been missed.

intro: This section catches any remaining deductions that may not have come up in earlier sections. Work through each carefully — some of the most valuable deductions in the T1 are here.

screens.title: Did you make charitable donations in 2025?
screens.instruction: Donations to registered Canadian charities qualify for a non-refundable tax credit under s.118.1. The first $200 generates a 15% credit federally — amounts above $200 generate a 29% credit (33% for high-income taxpayers). Combining donations with a spouse and carrying forward up to 5 years maximizes the above-$200 threshold.

options.label: Yes — cash or credit card donations to registered charities
Yes — I donated publicly traded securities directly to a charity
Both — cash and securities
Yes — through payroll deduction at work
No charitable donations in 2025

cash_donations.message: Charitable donation credit confirmed under s.118.1. The federal credit rate above $200 is 29% — or 33% if your income exceeds $246,752 in 2025. Combining all family donations on one return and maximizing above the $200 threshold is more efficient than splitting between spouses. Carry forward unused amounts up to 5 years.

document_checklist_add.document: Official charitable donation receipts — must show charity registration number, date, and amount. Online confirmation emails are acceptable if they include these details.

securities.message: In-kind securities donation confirmed — this is one of the most tax-efficient donation strategies available. When publicly traded securities with an accrued gain are donated directly to a registered charity, the capital gains inclusion rate is reduced to zero under s.38(a.1) — meaning no capital gains tax on the appreciation AND you receive the full fair market value donation receipt. Selling the securities and donating cash is far less efficient.

document_checklist_add.document: Charity receipt for in-kind securities donation — must show FMV at date of transfer
Brokerage transfer confirmation — shows number of shares, date of transfer, and FMV
Original purchase records for donated securities — confirms ACB for capital gain calculation

preparer_flag.flag: In-kind securities donation — zero inclusion rate under s.38(a.1). Confirm FMV receipt from charity and brokerage transfer confirmation. Do not report capital gain on donated securities.

payroll.message: Payroll deduction donations confirmed. Your employer should provide a T4 or separate summary showing total payroll donations. Combined with any direct donations, all amounts contribute to the above-$200 threshold for the higher credit rate.

document_checklist_add.document: Employer payroll donation summary — total 2025 payroll deductions for charitable giving. May be on T4 or separate letter.

screens.title: Did you have unused charitable donation amounts carried forward from prior years?
screens.instruction: Unused charitable donations can be carried forward up to 5 years. If you had large donations in prior years that generated more credit than you could use, the balance carries forward.

options.label: Yes
No
Not sure

true.message: Prior year donation carryforward confirmed. Bring your most recent Notice of Assessment — it shows your donation carryforward balance. Combined with 2025 donations, your preparer will maximize the amount above the $200 threshold for the higher credit rate.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — shows donation carryforward balance. 5-year limit applies — check oldest year does not expire.

preparer_flag.flag: Donation carryforward — check expiry year. 5-year limit means 2020 donations expire in 2025. Apply oldest carryforward first.

unsure.message: Check your most recent Notice of Assessment — donation carryforward is listed there. Given the 5-year expiry, donations from 2020 expire in 2025 — worth confirming before they lapse.

preparer_flag.flag: Donation carryforward uncertain — check NOA. 2020 donations expire in 2025 — apply before lapse.

screens.title: Did your spouse also make charitable donations in 2025?
screens.instruction: Combining all family charitable donations on one return is always more efficient — the higher credit rate above $200 applies to the combined total rather than each spouse having a separate $200 base amount.

options.label: Yes — spouse also donated
No — only my donations

true.message: Combining all family donations on one return confirmed as optimal. Bring both spouses' donation receipts to the same appointment — your preparer will determine which spouse's return generates the highest combined credit based on income levels.

preparer_flag.flag: Combine both spouses' donations on higher-income spouse return for maximum credit above $200 threshold. Confirm which return after both net incomes calculated.

document_checklist_add.document: Both spouses' charitable receipts — bring all to same appointment for combined claim optimization

screens.title: Did you or a family member incur significant medical expenses in 2025 — beyond what was already covered in the disability section?
screens.instruction: Medical expenses are one of the most broadly applicable and most commonly under-claimed items in the T1. The 12-month window rule means you can choose any 12 consecutive months ending in 2025 — not just January to December 2025.

options.label: Yes — dental, prescription drugs, physiotherapy, vision, or other medical costs
No significant medical expenses

true.message: Medical expenses confirmed. The credit is 15% of eligible expenses above the lesser of 3% of net income or $2,759 under s.118.2. Claim on the lower-income spouse's return for a lower 3% threshold. Choose the optimal 12-month window with your preparer.

document_checklist_add.document: All medical receipts with dates — pharmacy annual summary, dental statements, physiotherapy receipts, vision receipts. Dates matter for 12-month window optimization.

screens.title: Did you make contributions to a federal political party or candidate in 2025?
screens.instruction: Federal political contributions generate a non-refundable tax credit under s.127(3) — 75% of the first $400, 50% of the next $350, and 33.3% of amounts between $750 and $1,275. Maximum credit is $650.

options.label: Yes — to a registered federal party or candidate
Yes — to a provincial party or candidate only
No political contributions

true.message: Federal political contribution credit confirmed under s.127(3). Your political party or Elections Canada will issue an official receipt — unlike charitable donations, political receipts are issued by the party or candidate, not just by CRA-registered entities.

document_checklist_add.document: Official political contribution receipts — issued by federal party or candidate. Must show amount and contributor name.

provincial.message: Provincial political contributions generate a provincial tax credit — not a federal credit. The credit is calculated on your provincial return. In Ontario, the political contribution credit is available under the Ontario Election Finances Act. Bring your receipt.

document_checklist_add.document: Provincial political contribution receipt — issued by provincial party or candidate

screens.title: Did you borrow money to invest in 2025?
screens.instruction: Interest paid on money borrowed to earn investment income is deductible under s.20(1)(c). This includes margin loans, investment lines of credit, and loans used to purchase income-producing investments — but not loans used to purchase RRSP or TFSA contributions.

options.label: Yes — I used borrowed funds to purchase investments
No

true.message: Investment interest deduction confirmed under s.20(1)(c). Interest is deductible only if the borrowed funds were used to earn income — interest from investments, dividends, or business income. Interest on loans to purchase growth stocks that pay no dividends is a grey area — your preparer will confirm eligibility. The deduction is not available for RRSP or TFSA loans.

document_checklist_add.document: Annual interest statement from lender — total interest paid in 2025 on investment loans
Evidence that borrowed funds were used to purchase income-producing investments — trade confirmations or brokerage statements

preparer_flag.flag: Investment interest deduction — confirm borrowed funds used for income-producing investments under s.20(1)(c). Growth-only investments may not qualify. RRSP/TFSA loans not deductible.

screens.title: Did you pay any of these investment-related fees in 2025?
screens.instruction: Certain investment management fees and carrying charges are deductible under s.20(1)(bb) and related provisions. Note that fees deducted inside a registered plan are not deductible separately.

options.label: Investment management or advisory fees — paid directly outside the account
Safety deposit box rental fees — used to store investment documents
Accounting fees for investment income reporting
None of the above

investment_counsel.message: Investment management fees paid directly outside a registered account may be deductible under s.20(1)(bb). Note: CRA has narrowed this deduction in recent years — trailing commissions and embedded fees are generally not deductible. Fees charged directly by invoice from an investment manager for non-registered accounts are the clearest case. Your preparer will confirm eligibility.

document_checklist_add.document: Investment management fee invoices — direct fees paid outside registered accounts only. Embedded MER fees are not separately deductible.

preparer_flag.flag: Investment counsel fees — confirm eligibility under s.20(1)(bb). Direct invoiced fees for non-registered accounts qualify. Trailing commissions and embedded fees do not.

safety_deposit.message: Safety deposit box fees are deductible under s.20(1)(bb) if the box is used to store investment documents or income-producing property. The amount is modest but worth including.

document_checklist_add.document: Safety deposit box fee receipt — annual fee from financial institution

accounting_investment.message: Accounting fees paid to prepare returns or for advice related to investment income may be deductible under s.20(1)(bb). General tax return preparation fees are not deductible for individuals — only fees specifically related to investment income reporting qualify. Your preparer will identify the deductible portion.

preparer_flag.flag: Accounting fees — deductible only for investment income advisory portion under s.20(1)(bb). General T1 preparation fees not deductible for individuals.

screens.title: Did you have employment income subject to CPP contributions in 2025?
screens.instruction: The enhanced CPP contribution — the difference between the enhanced rate and the base rate — is deductible as an other deduction under s.60(e.1). This is calculated automatically from your T4 but is frequently missed when preparers use older software.

options.label: Yes — I had T4 employment income in 2025
No T4 employment income

true.message: CPP enhanced contribution deduction confirmed under s.60(e.1). For 2025 the deductible amount is 1% of pensionable earnings between $3,500 and $71,300 plus the full CPP2 amount. Your T4 Box 16 and Box 16A show your CPP contributions — your preparer will calculate the deductible portion automatically.

screens.title: Did you have any employment expenses we have not yet covered — such as professional dues, uniforms, or licences required for work?
screens.instruction: This is a catch-all for employment expenses not covered in the employment section earlier.

options.label: Professional dues or licensing fees required for my occupation
Legal fees to collect wages or wrongful dismissal
Cost of musical instruments required for employment as a musician
No additional employment expenses

professional_dues.message: Professional dues and licensing fees required for your occupation are deductible under s.8(1)(i). This includes annual dues to professional associations — engineers, acuntants, nurses, lawyers — where membership is required to practice. These often appear on your T4 in Box 44 but may need to be claimed separately if not on the T4.

document_checklist_add.document: Professional association receipts or T4 Box 44 — confirm if amount already on T4 or needs separate receipt

legal_work.message: Legal fees to collect wages owed by an employer or for wrongful dismissal proceedings are deductible under s.8(1)(b). Note: legal fees for employment insurance appeals or pension benefit appeals are also deductible under s.60(o.1). Keep all legal invoices with descriptions of services.

document_checklist_add.document: Legal invoices for employment matter — description of services must show wages collection or wrongful dismissal context

musical_instruments.message: Musicians employed as employees may deduct the cost of musical instruments under s.8(1)(p). The deduction is limited to employment income earned as a musician. Keep purchase receipts and maintenance costs — a Form T2200 from your employer confirming the instrument requirement is needed.

document_checklist_add.document: Musical instrument purchase receipts and maintenance costs plus Form T2200 from employer

screens.title: Did you pay fees to object to or appeal a tax assessment in 2025?
screens.instruction: Legal and professional fees paid to file a notice of objection or appeal to the Tax Court of Canada are deductible under s.60(o).

options.label: Yes — I paid fees to object to or appeal a CRA assessment
No

true.message: Tax objection and appeal fees are deductible under s.60(o in the year paid — not the year the objection is resolved. Keep your legal invoices showing the fees relate to a tax matter specifically.

document_checklist_add.document: Legal invoices for tax objection or appeal — must show services related to CRA assessment or Tax Court matter

screens.title: Did you live in a prescribed northern or intermediate zone in 2025?
screens.instruction: Residents of prescribed northern zones — including parts of northern Ontario — may claim the Northern Residents Deduction under s.110.7 for the additional cost of living in remote areas.

options.label: Yes — prescribed northern zone (Zone A)
Yes — prescribed intermediate zone (Zone B)
NI lived in a southern area

northern_zone.message: Northern Residents Deduction confirmed for Zone A under s.110.7. Two components apply: the basic residency amount of $11 per day plus an additional travel benefit for travel to access medical or vacation travel. You must have lived in the zone for at least 6 consecutive months beginning or ending in 2025.

document_checklist_add.document: Proof of northern zone residence — utility bills, lease, or other evidence of address in prescribed zone. Travel receipts if claiming travel benefit.

preparer_flag.flag: Northern Residents Deduction Zone A — confirm 6 consecutive months residency and calculate basic plus travel components under s.110.7.

intermediate_zone.message: Northern Residents Deduction confirmed for Zone B — intermediate zone. The deduction is half of the Zone A amount. Same 6-month residency requirement applies.

document_checklist_add.document: Proof of Zone B residence — same documentation as Zone A. Deduction is 50% of Zone A amount.

screens.title: Did you pay spousal suppo in 2025 that was not already covered in the marital status section?
screens.instruction: This is a catch-all for taxpayers who pay spousal support but did not select marital status change as a life event — for example, if the separation occurred in a prior year.

options.label: Yes — I pay ongoing spsal support under a prior agreement
No

true.message: Ongoing spousal support deduction confirmed under s.60(b). Bring your separation agreement or court order and proof of payments made in 2025.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order — confirms support terms and amount
Proof of 2025 payments — bank statements or cancelled cheques

screens.title: Do you have your most recent Notice of Assessment available?
screens.instruction: Your most recent Notice of Assessment is the single most important carry-forward document for your 2025 return. It shows your RRSP room, tuition carryforward, donation carryforward, capital loss carryforward, HBP balance, and LLP balance all in one place.

options.label: Yes — I have my 2024 NOA
I can access it through CRA My Account
No — I do not have it

true.message: NOA confirmed. Your preparer will use it to confirm all carryforward balances. Bring the most recent one — your 2024 NOA received in 2025 is the relevant document.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — most important carryforward document. Shows RRSP room, tuition credit, donations, capital losses, HBP and LLP balances.

online.message: CRA My Account access confirmed. Download or screenshot your most recent NOA before your preparer appointment. CRA My Account also shows real-time RRSP and TFSA room which may be more current than the NOA.

document_checklist_add.document: Download 2024 NOA from CRA My Account before appointment. Also note RRSP and TFSA room shown in real time.

false.message: Request a copy from CRA My Account or call CRA at 1-800-959-8281 to request a copy by mail. Your preparer may be able to retrieve it directly from CRA with your authorization. Without it, several carryforward amounts may be missed.

preparer_flag.flag: No NOA available — retrieve from CRA with taxpayer authorization. Critical for RRSP room, tuition, donations, capital loss carryforwards.

screens.title: Do you have net capital losses carried forward from prior years?
screens.instruction: Net capital losses from prior years can be applied against taxable capital gains in 2025. They can also be carried back 3 years if you had capital gains in 2022, 2023, or 2024.

options.label: Yes — I have capital losses from prior years
No
Not sure — I had investment losses in prior years

true.message: Capital loss carryforward confirmed. Net capital losses carried forward under s.111((b) can be applied against 2025 taxable capital gains. Your most recent NOA shows the carryforward balance.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent NOA — shows net capital loss carryforward balance available to offset 2025 gains

unsure.message: Check your most recent NOA — net capital loss carryforward is listed there. stment losses only carry forward as capital losses if they were realized (sold) — unrealized losses on investments you still hold do not generate a carryforward.

preparer_flag.flag: Capital loss carryforward uncertain — check NOA. Realized losses only — unrealized losses on held investments do not create carryforward.

screens.title: Is your net income near a threshold that affects benefits or credits you are receiving?
screens.instruction: Several federal and provincial benefits are income-tested. Small deductions can have outsized effects when they push net income below a threshold.

options.label: Near the OAS clawback threshold (~$90,997)
Near the Canada Child Benefit reduction zone
Near the Age Amount phase-out (~$44,325)
Near the LIFT Credit phase-out (~$30,000 individual)
Not near any specific threshold

oas_threshold.message: OAS clawback threshold sensitivity noted. Every deduction dollar below $90,997 saves both marginal income tax AND preserves OAS at 15 cents per dollar — an effective marginal saving rate sigficantly above the nominal tax rate. Your preparer should prioritize all available deductions with this in mind.

preparer_flag.flag: Net income near OAS clawback threshold — maximize all deductions to reduce below $90,997. Each deduction dollar saves income tax plus 15 cents of OAS recovery.

ccb_threshold.message: Canada Child Benefit income sensitivity noted. CCB begins phasing out above $36,502 for one child (2025 figures). Deductions that reduce net income below phase-out zones directly increase CCB payments — a meaningful cash flow benefit for families with children Your preparer should model the CCB impact of each deduction.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB income threshold sensitivity — model effect of deductions on CCB entitlement. Deductions below phase-out threshold increase CCB cash payments.

age_amount_threshold.message: Age Amount phase-out sensitivity noted. The Age Amount reduces by 15 cents for each dollar of net income above $44,325. Deductions that keep net income below this threshold preserve the full Age Amount credit.

preparer_flag.flag: Net income near Age Amount phase-out — maximize deductions to preserve full $1,259 federal Age Amount credit. Phase-out starts at $44,325.

lift_threshold.message: LIFT Credit threshold sensitivity noted. Reducing net income below $30,000 individual or $60,000 family preserves the full LIFT Credit. RRSP contributions are the most effective tool for reducing net income to this threshold.

preparer_flag.flag: LIFT Credit threshold sensitivity — model RRSP contribution to reduce net income below $30,000 individual threshold. LIFT worth up to $875 annually.

branch_completn_summary.message: Deductions review complete. Here is the running checklist of everything identified so far:
highlighted_documents: All charitable donation receipts — official receipts with charity registration number. Combine with spouse's receipts.
All medical receipts with dates — pharmacy summary, dental statements, physiotherapy,ion. Dates essential for 12-month window.
Most recent Notice of Assessment — single most important carry-forward document. Shows all balances.
Investment loan interest statements if applicable.
Political contribution receipts if applicable.
Northern residents zone documentation if applicable.
Professional dues receipts not on T4.
branch_completn_summary.transition: Final section: we'll do a comphensive sweep of credits to make sure everything is captured.

intro: We're almost done. This final section confirms the credits that apply to your situation — including several that are automatically calculated but worth verifying, and Ontario-specific credits that may apply to you.

screens.title: Is this a straightforward year — no bankruptcy, no departure from Canada, and no period as a non-resident?
screens.instruction: The Basic Personal Amount is a non-refundable credit claimed by virtually every Canadian resident — $16,129 federally in 2025. However it is prorated for part-year residents and modified in certain situations.

options.label: Yes — full year Canadian resident with no unusual circumstances
No — I became a Canadian resident or left Canada part-way through 2025
No — I declared bankruptcy in 2025

true.message: Full Basic Personal Amount of $16,129 confirmed for 2025 under s.118(1)(c). Applied automatically on your return — no action required.

part_year.message: Part-year residency noted. The Basic Personal Amount is prorated based on days of Canadian residency under s.114. Your preparer will calculate the correct amount from your arrival or departure date.

preparer_flag.flag: Part-year residency — prorate Basic Personal Amount under s.114. Confirm arrival or departure date for calculation.

bankruptcy.message: Bankruptcy noted. A taxpayer who declares bankruptcy files two returns in the bankruptcy year — a pre-bankruptcy return and a post-bankruptcy return. Credits and deductions are allocated between the two periods. This requires specialized preparer knowledge — flag immediately.

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: Bankruptcy declared in 2025 — two returns required. Pre-bankruptcy and post-bankruptcy returns filed separately. Specialized treatment required.

screens.title: Does your spouse or common-law partner have net income below $16,129 for 2025?
screens.instruction: The Spousal Amount credit under s.118(1)(a) is available when your spouse's net income is below the Basic Personal Amount. It is reduced dollar-for-dollar by their income.

options.label: Yes — spouse has little or no income
Spouse has some income — between $1 and $16,129
No — spouse has income above $16,129

true.message: Full Spousal Amount confirmed under s.118(1)(a) — worth $2,421 federally in 2025. Applied automatically when spouse's net income is reported on your return.

partial.message: Partial Spousal Amount applies — reduced by spouse's net income. Your preparer will calculate the exact amount once spouse's net income is confirmed.

screens.title: Do you have earned income from employment or self-employment and a net income below approximately $33,015?
screens.instruction: The Canada Workers Benefit is a refundable credit under s.122.7 — meaning it can generate a refund even if you owe no tax. Worth up to $1,518 for single individuals and $2,616 for families in 2025.

options.label: Yes — single, earned income present, net income under ~$33,015
Yes — family with spouse or dependents, earned income present
I have earned income but am not sure of my net income
No — income above threshold or no earned income

single_eligible.message: Canada Workers Benefit confirmed under s.122.7. Worth up to $1,518 for single individuals in 2025 — a refundable credit meaning you receive it even with no tax owing. Advanced payments may have been made quarterly by CRA — your preparer will reconcile advances against final entitlement.

preparer_flag.flag: CWB — confirm advance payments received in 2025 from CRA. Reconcile advances against final entitlement on Schedule 6.

family_eligible.message: Canada Workers Benefit family rate confirmed — worth up to $2,616 for families in 2025. Both spouses with earned income may claim — your preparer will determine the optimal split between returns.

unsure.message: CWB flagged as possible — your preparer will calculate eligibility once net income is confirmed. It is refundable so worth checking regardless.

screens.title: Are you registered to receive the GST/HST Credit?
screens.instruction: The GST/HST Credit is a quarterly tax-free payment for individuals and families with low or moderate incomes. It is applied automatically when you file your return — but newcomers must apply separately using Form RC151.

options.label: Yes — I receive quarterly GST/HST Credit payments
No — I am not receiving it but may qualify
I am a newcomer and have not yet applied
No — my income is above the threshold

receiving.message: GST/HST Credit confirmed. Payments are recalculated each July based on prior year net income — filing your return on time is essential to avoid interruption.

not_receiving_eligible.message: GST/HST Credit is applied automatically when you file your T1 return — no separate application required for existing residents. If you have been filing and not receiving it, CRA has determined your income is above the threshold. Your preparer will confirm eligibility based on 2025 net income.

newcomer.message: Newcomers must apply for the GST/HST Credit using Form RC151 — it is not automatic for new residents. Submit Form RC151 as soon as possible — payments are not retroactive beyond the application date.

preparer_flag.flag: Newcomer — GST/HST Credit requires Form RC151. Submit immediately — payments not retroactive beyond application.

document_checklist_add.document: Form RC151 — GST/HST Credit Application for Newcomers to Canada. Submit immediately.

screens.title: Did you receive dividends from Canadian corporations in 2025?

options.label: Yes — dividends from Canadian public or private corporations
No Canadian dividends

true.message: Dividend Tax Credit confirmed under s.121. Canadian dividends are grossed up on your return — eligible dividends by 38%, non-eligible by 15%. The Dividend Tax Credit partially offsets the gross-up. Your T5 slip shows both the actual dividend and the grossed-up amount. Bring all T5 slips.

document_checklist_add.document: T5 slips — Statement of Investment Income. Shows actual dividend, grossed-up amount, and dividend tax credit amount.

screens.title: Did you pay tax to a foreign country on income that is also taxable in Canada?

options.label: Yes — on foreign business income
Yes — on foreign investment income, pension, or employment income
Both business and non-business foreign income
No foreign taxes paid

business_income.message: Foreign tax credit for business income confirmed under s.126(2). Credits for foreign business income taxes are calculated separately from non-business credits and have a 10-year carryforward. Bring your foreign tax assessment or withholding certificate.

document_checklist_add.document: Foreign tax assessment or withholding certificate — confirms amount of foreign tax paid on business income

non_business.message: Foreign tax credit for non-business income confirmed under s.126(1). Applies to foreign withholding on dividends, interest, pensions, and employment income. Limited to 15% on most treaty countries for passive income. Excess foreign tax not creditable may be deductible as an expense.

document_checklist_add.document: Foreign tax withholding certificates or NR4 slips — confirms amount of foreign tax withheld on each income source

screens.title: Did you make eligible renovations to your home in 2025 to improve accessibility or safety for a senior or person with a disability?
screens.instruction: The Home Accessibility Tax Credit under s.118.041 provides a 15% non-refundable credit on up to $20,000 of eligible renovation costs — worth up to $3,000 federally. The renovations must allow a qualifying individual to access or be mobile in the home.

options.label: Yes — ramps, grab bars, stair lifts, wider doorways, or similar modifications
No accessibility renovations

true.message: Home Accessibility Tax Credit confirmed under s.118.041. Maximum eligible expenditure is $20,000 — worth up to $3,000 in federal tax reduction. Eligible renovations include wheelchair ramps, grab bars, stair lifts, walk-in tubs, wider doorways, and non-slip flooring. The renovation must be of an enduring nature — not temporary equipment.

document_checklist_add.document: Contractor invoices for accessibility renovations — description of work, date, and amount. Must be permanent modifications not equipment.

preparer_flag.flag: Home Accessibility Credit — confirm qualifying individual (age 65+ or DTC-eligible) and that renovation is enduring under s.118.041. Max $20,000 eligible expenditure.

screens.title: Did you complete a renovation in 2025 to create a secondary dwelling unit for a senior family member or adult with a disability?
screens.instruction: The Multigenerational Home Renovation Tax Credit under s.118.05 provides a 15% refundable credit on up to $50,000 of eligible renovation costs — worth up to $7,500. The unit must be a self-contained suite within the home for a qualifying senior (65+) or DTC-eligible individual.

options.label: Yes — I created a secondary suite for an elderly parent or disabled family member
No

true.message: Multigenerational Home Renovation Tax Credit confirmed under s.118.05. This is a refundable credit — worth up to $7,500 regardless of tax owing. The suite must be self-contained with its own kitchen, bathroom, and sleeping area. Claim once per lifetime per qualifying occupant. Cannot be combined with the First Home Buyers' Tax Credit for the same renovation.

document_checklist_add.document: All renovation invoices for secondary suite construction — must total eligible costs and show completion in 2025. Building permit if required.

preparer_flag.flag: Multigenerational Renovation Credit — confirm suite is self-contained, qualifying individual will occupy, and credit not claimed before for same person. Refundable — max $7,500.

screens.title: Did you volunteer as a firefighter or search and rescue worker in 2025?
screens.instruction: Volunteer firefighters and search and rescue volunteers who perform at least 200 hours of eligible service are entitled to a $3,000 tax credit amount under s.118.06 and s.118.07 — worth $450 federally.

options.label: Yes — volunteer firefighter
Yes — volunteer search and rescue worker
Both
No

firefighter.message: Volunteer Firefighter Tax Credit confirmed under s.118.06. You must have performed at least 200 hours of eligible volunteer service in 2025. The fire department should provide a letter confirming your hours.

document_checklist_add.document: Letter from fire department confirming volunteer service hours in 2025 — minimum 200 hours required.

search_rescue.message: Search and Rescue Volunteer Tax Credit confirmed under s.118.07. Same 200-hour minimum applies. Obtain confirmation letter from your search and rescue organization.

document_checklist_add.document: Letter from search and rescue organization confirming volunteer hours in 2025 — minimum 200 hours.

both.message: Both credits noted. Important — you can claim both the Volunteer Firefighter Credit and the Search and Rescue Volunteer Credit if you meet the 200-hour threshold for each separately. However combined hours cannot be used to satisfy both thresholds — each requires its own 200 hours.

preparer_flag.flag: Both volunteer credits cimed — confirm 200 hours each separately. Hours cannot be shared between the two credits.

screens.title: Did you pay for a digital news subscription in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Digital News Subscription Credit under s.118.02 provides a 15% credit on up to $500 of eligible digital news subscriptions — worth up to $75 federally. The publication must be a qualified Canadian journalism organization.

options.label: Yes — Globe and Mail, Toronto Star, National Post, or other Canadian digital news
No

true.message: Digital News Subscription Credit onfirmed under s.118.02. Maximum credit is $75 federally on $500 of subscriptions. The publication must be a qualified Canadian journalism organization — international publications like the New York Times do notualify. Your subscription invoice or credit card statement is sufficient.

document_checklist_add.document: Digital news subscription receipts or credit card statements — Canadian publications only. Annual total for all qualifying subscriptions.

screens.title: Did you pay rent or property tax for your principal                residence in Ontario in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Ontario Trillium Benefit combines three credits into monthly payments — the Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit, the Northern Ontario Energy Cdit, and the Ontario Sales Tax Credit. Claimed on Form ON-BEN filed with your Ontario return.

options.label: Yes — I paid rent for an Ontario residence
Yes — I paid property tax on an Ontario home I own
Both — I rented part of the year and owned part of the year
I live in subsidized housing — no direct rent or property tax
No — I did not pay rent or property tax in Ontario

rent.message: Ontario Trillium Benefit — Energy and Property Tax Credit (rental component) confirmed under OTA s.103.1. The credit is based on 20% of rent paid up to a maximum. Your landlord must be at arm's length — rent paid to a related person may not qualify. Claim on Form ON-BEN.

document_checklist_add.document: Rent receipts for 2025 — landlord name, address, monthly rent amount. Annual total required for Form ON-BEN.

property_tax.message: Ontario Trillium Benefit — Energy and Property Tax Credit (homeowner component) confirmed. Based on property tax paid. Your annual property tax bill shows the amount paid. Claim on Form ON-BEN.

document_checklist_add.document: 2025 property tax bill — total property tax paid for the year. Municipal tax bill or mortgage statement showing property tax.

both.message: Mixed rental and ownership year noted. Claim both components on Form ON-BEN with the appropriate amounts and dates for each period.

document_checklist_add.document: Rent receipts for rental period — with dates
Property tax bill for ownership period — prorated if mid-year purchase

subsidized.message: Residents of subsidized housing, nursing homes, or long-term care filities may still qualify for a portion of the OTB based on occupancy cost. Your facility can provide a statement showing your 2025 occupancy cost.

document_checklist_add.document: Occupancy cost statement from facility — confirms amounts paid for ON-BEN calculation

screens.title: Did yolive in Northern Ontario in 2025?
screens.instruction: Residents of Northern Ontario may qualify for the Northern Ontario Energy Credit component of the OTB — an additional amount reflecting higher energy costs in the north.

options.label: Yes — I lived in Northern Ontario (north of the French River / Nipissing District line)
No — Southern Ontario

true.message: Northern Ontario Energy Credit component of OTB confirmed under OTA s.103.1. Worth up to $174 for individuals and $266 for families in 2025. Claimed on Form ON-BEN along with the main OTB.

screens.title: Confirming LIFT Credit eligibility — was your individual net income in 2025 below approximately $50,000?
screens.instruction: The Ontario LIFT Credit reduces or eliminates Ontario personal income tax for low and middle-income workers. Maximum credit is $875. Phases out between $30,000 and $50,000 individual net income and between $60,000 and $82,500 family net income.

options.label: Yes — under $30,000 individual net income
Possibly — between $30,000 and $50,000
No — above $50,000 individual net income

under_30k.message: Full LIFT Credit confirmed under OTA s.103.4. Worth up to $875 in 2025. Calculated automatically from your Ontario return — no separate application required. Ensure your preparer confirms it is applied.

preparer_flag.flag: LIFT Credit confirmed — calculated automatically on Ontario return. Verify it appears on Form ON428. Commonly missed by newcomers with partial-year income.

30k_to_50k.message: Partial LIFT Credit possible — phase-out applies between $30,000 and $50,000 individual net income. Family net income threshold is $60,000 to $82,500. Your preparer will calculate the exact amount from your net income figures.

screens.title: Are you 64 or older and a homeowner in Ontario?
screens.instruction: The Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant provides up to $500 annually for eligible seniors under OTA s.104.1. Income thresholds apply.

options.label: Yes — age 64 or older and own my Ontario home
No

true.message: Ontio Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant confirmed under OTA s.104.1. Worth up to $500 for individual net income below $35,000 — phases out to $50,000. Claimed on Form ON-BEN with your Ontario return. Your property tax bill is the required document.

document_checklist_add.document: 2025 property tax bill — confirms ownership and tax paid for Ontario Senior Grant on Form ON-BEN

screens.title: Did you provide care for an elderly or infirm family member in your home in 2025 that was not already covered?

options.label: Yes — I supported an elderly or infirm relative in my household
No

true.message: Canada Caregiver Amount flagged for review under s.118(1)(c.1). Your preparer will confirm eligibility based on the dependent's net income and infirmity status. Maximum credit is $7,999 federally for 2025 for an infirm dependent living with you.

document_checklist_add.document: Dependent's net income information for 2025 — required for caregiver credit calculation

screens.title: Did you receive any eligible pension income in 2025 that qualifies for the Pensi Income Credit?

options.label: Yes — RRIF, annuity, or employer pension
No eligible pension income

true.message: Pension Income Credit confirmed under s.118(3). Worth up to $300 federally in 2025 — 15% of the first $2,000 of eligible pension income.                      Applied automatically from your pension slips. Ensure your preparer confirms it is applied — it is sometimes missed in the first year of retirement.

screens.title: Before we generate your T1 preparation brief, confirm you have or will gather the following universal documents:
screens.instruction: These documents apply to virtually every taxpayer and are required regardless of your specific situation.

options.label: Social Insurance Number (SIN)
All T4 slips from employers
T5 and other investment slips if applicable
Most recent Notice of Assessment
Banking information for direct deposit of any refund
Government-issued ID if first-time filing with this preparer

default.message: Universal documents noted. These form the baseline for every T1 return. Make sure all T4 slips are collected — CRA receives copies from all employers and will flag any missing T4 income even if you forget to report it.

screens.title: As a newcomer to Canada, confirm you have addressed these newcomer-specific items:
screens.instruction: These items are particularly important for newcomers and first-year filers. Select each you have confirmed.

options.label: I understand I must report worldwide income from my arrival date onward
I have reviewed foreign asset reporting requirements (T1135 if foreign property over $100,000)
I have applied for the Canada Child Benefit if I have children (Forms RC66 and RC66SCH)
I have applied for the GST/HST Credit (Form RC151)
I understand my TFSA room starts from my arrival year — not 29

foreign_assets.message: Foreign asset reporting is critical. If you hold foreign property with a cost base exceeding $100,000 CAD — including foreign bank accounts, investments, or real este — you must file Form T1135 annually. Failure to file carries significant penalties — $25 per day up to $2,500 plus potential gross negligence penalties.

preparer_flag.flag: Newcomer foreign assets — confirm T1135 requirement if foreign property cost exceeds $100,000 CAD. Penalties for non-filing are significant.

branch_completion_summary.message: Credits review complete. Your T1 preparation brief is now ready.
